speeches.speech: If music be the food of love, play on; Give me excess of it, that, surfeiting, The appetite may sicken, and so die. That strain again! it had a dying fall: O, it came o'er my ear like the sweet sound, That breathes upon a bank of violets, Stealing and giving odour! Enough; no more: 'Tis not so sweet now as it was before. O spirit of love! how quick and fresh art thou, That, notwithstanding thy capacity Receiveth as the sea, nought enters there, Of what validity and pitch soe'er, But falls into abatement and low price, Even in a minute: so full of shapes is fancy That it alone is high fantastical.
lines: If music be the food of love, play on;
Give me excess of it, that, surfeiting,
The appetite may sicken, and so die.
That strain again! it had a dying fall:
O, it came o'er my ear like the sweet sound,
That breathes upon a bank of violets,
Stealing and giving odour! Enough; no more:
'Tis not so sweet now as it was before.
O spirit of love! how quick and fresh art thou,
That, notwithstanding thy capacity
Receiveth as the sea, nought enters there,
Of what validity and pitch soe'er,
But falls into abatement and low price,
Even in a minute: so full of shapes is fancy
That it alone is high fantastical.

speeches.speech: Will you go hunt, my lord?
lines: Will you go hunt, my lord?

speeches.speech: What, Curio?
lines: What, Curio?

speeches.speech: The hart.
lines: The hart.

speeches.speech: Why, so I do, the noblest that I have: O, when mine eyes did see Olivia first, Methought she purged the air of pestilence! That instant was I turn'd into a hart; And my desires, like fell and cruel hounds, E'er since pursue me. [Enter VALENTINE] How now! what news from her?
lines: Why, so I do, the noblest that I have:
O, when mine eyes did see Olivia first,
Methought she purged the air of pestilence!
That instant was I turn'd into a hart;
And my desires, like fell and cruel hounds,
E'er since pursue me.
[Enter VALENTINE]
How now! what news from her?

speeches.speech: So please my lord, I might not be admitted; But from her handmaid do return this answer: The element itself, till seven years' heat, Shall not behold her face at ample view; But, like a cloistress, she will veiled walk And water once a day her chamber round With eye-offending brine: all this to season A brother's dead love, which she would keep fresh And lasting in her sad remembrance.
lines: So please my lord, I might not be admitted;
But from her handmaid do return this answer:
The element itself, till seven years' heat,
Shall not behold her face at ample view;
But, like a cloistress, she will veiled walk
And water once a day her chamber round
With eye-offending brine: all this to season
A brother's dead love, which she would keep fresh
And lasting in her sad remembrance.

speeches.speech: O, she that hath a heart of that fine frame To pay this debt of love but to a brother, How will she love, when the rich golden shaft Hath kill'd the flock of all affections else That live in her; when liver, brain and heart, These sovereign thrones, are all supplied, and fill'd Her sweet perfections with one self king! Away before me to sweet beds of flowers: Love-thoughts lie rich when canopied with bowers.
lines: O, she that hath a heart of that fine frame
To pay this debt of love but to a brother,
How will she love, when the rich golden shaft
Hath kill'd the flock of all affections else
That live in her; when liver, brain and heart,
These sovereign thrones, are all supplied, and fill'd
Her sweet perfections with one self king!
Away before me to sweet beds of flowers:
Love-thoughts lie rich when canopied with bowers.

speeches.speech: What country, friends, is this?
lines: What country, friends, is this?

speeches.speech: This is Illyria, lady.
lines: This is Illyria, lady.

speeches.speech: And what should I do in Illyria? My brother he is in Elysium. Perchance he is not drown'd: what think you, sailors?
lines: And what should I do in Illyria?
My brother he is in Elysium.
Perchance he is not drown'd: what think you, sailors?

speeches.speech: It is perchance that you yourself were saved.
lines: It is perchance that you yourself were saved.

speeches.speech: O my poor brother! and so perchance may he be.
lines: O my poor brother! and so perchance may he be.

speeches.speech: True, madam: and, to comfort you with chance, Assure yourself, after our ship did split, When you and those poor number saved with you Hung on our driving boat, I saw your brother, Most provident in peril, bind himself, Courage and hope both teaching him the practise, To a strong mast that lived upon the sea; Where, like Arion on the dolphin's back, I saw him hold acquaintance with the waves So long as I could see.
lines: True, madam: and, to comfort you with chance,
Assure yourself, after our ship did split,
When you and those poor number saved with you
Hung on our driving boat, I saw your brother,
Most provident in peril, bind himself,
Courage and hope both teaching him the practise,
To a strong mast that lived upon the sea;
Where, like Arion on the dolphin's back,
I saw him hold acquaintance with the waves
So long as I could see.

speeches.speech: For saying so, there's gold: Mine own escape unfoldeth to my hope, Whereto thy speech serves for authority, The like of him. Know'st thou this country?
lines: For saying so, there's gold:
Mine own escape unfoldeth to my hope,
Whereto thy speech serves for authority,
The like of him. Know'st thou this country?

speeches.speech: Ay, madam, well; for I was bred and born Not three hours' travel from this very place.
lines: Ay, madam, well; for I was bred and born
Not three hours' travel from this very place.

speeches.speech: Who governs here?
lines: Who governs here?

speeches.speech: A noble duke, in nature as in name.
lines: A noble duke, in nature as in name.

speeches.speech: What is the name?
lines: What is the name?

speeches.speech: Orsino.
lines: Orsino.

speeches.speech: Orsino! I have heard my father name him: He was a bachelor then.
lines: Orsino! I have heard my father name him:
He was a bachelor then.

speeches.speech: And so is now, or was so very late; For but a month ago I went from hence, And then 'twas fresh in murmur,--as, you know, What great ones do the less will prattle of,-- That he did seek the love of fair Olivia.
lines: And so is now, or was so very late;
For but a month ago I went from hence,
And then 'twas fresh in murmur,--as, you know,
What great ones do the less will prattle of,--
That he did seek the love of fair Olivia.

speeches.speech: What's she?
lines: What's she?

speeches.speech: A virtuous maid, the daughter of a count That died some twelvemonth since, then leaving her In the protection of his son, her brother, Who shortly also died: for whose dear love, They say, she hath abjured the company And sight of men.
lines: A virtuous maid, the daughter of a count
That died some twelvemonth since, then leaving her
In the protection of his son, her brother,
Who shortly also died: for whose dear love,
They say, she hath abjured the company
And sight of men.

speeches.speech: O that I served that lady And might not be delivered to the world, Till I had made mine own occasion mellow, What my estate is!
lines: O that I served that lady
And might not be delivered to the world,
Till I had made mine own occasion mellow,
What my estate is!

speeches.speech: That were hard to compass; Because she will admit no kind of suit, No, not the duke's.
lines: That were hard to compass;
Because she will admit no kind of suit,
No, not the duke's.

speeches.speech: There is a fair behavior in thee, captain; And though that nature with a beauteous wall Doth oft close in pollution, yet of thee I will believe thou hast a mind that suits With this thy fair and outward character. I prithee, and I'll pay thee bounteously, Conceal me what I am, and be my aid For such disguise as haply shall become The form of my intent. I'll serve this duke: Thou shall present me as an eunuch to him: It may be worth thy pains; for I can sing And speak to him in many sorts of music That will allow me very worth his service. What else may hap to time I will commit; Only shape thou thy silence to my wit.
lines: There is a fair behavior in thee, captain;
And though that nature with a beauteous wall
Doth oft close in pollution, yet of thee
I will believe thou hast a mind that suits
With this thy fair and outward character.
I prithee, and I'll pay thee bounteously,
Conceal me what I am, and be my aid
For such disguise as haply shall become
The form of my intent. I'll serve this duke:
Thou shall present me as an eunuch to him:
It may be worth thy pains; for I can sing
And speak to him in many sorts of music
That will allow me very worth his service.
What else may hap to time I will commit;
Only shape thou thy silence to my wit.

speeches.speech: Be you his eunuch, and your mute I'll be: When my tongue blabs, then let mine eyes not see.
lines: Be you his eunuch, and your mute I'll be:
When my tongue blabs, then let mine eyes not see.

speeches.speech: I thank thee: lead me on.
lines: I thank thee: lead me on.

speeches.speech: What a plague means my niece, to take the death of her brother thus? I am sure care's an enemy to life.
lines: What a plague means my niece, to take the death of
her brother thus? I am sure care's an enemy to life.

speeches.speech: By my troth, Sir Toby, you must come in earlier o' nights: your cousin, my lady, takes great exceptions to your ill hours.
lines: By my troth, Sir Toby, you must come in earlier o'
nights: your cousin, my lady, takes great
exceptions to your ill hours.

speeches.speech: Why, let her except, before excepted.
lines: Why, let her except, before excepted.

speeches.speech: Ay, but you must confine yourself within the modest limits of order.
lines: Ay, but you must confine yourself within the modest
limits of order.

speeches.speech: Confine! I'll confine myself no finer than I am: these clothes are good enough to drink in; and so be these boots too: an they be not, let them hang themselves in their own straps.
lines: Confine! I'll confine myself no finer than I am:
these clothes are good enough to drink in; and so be
these boots too: an they be not, let them hang
themselves in their own straps.

speeches.speech: That quaffing and drinking will undo you: I heard my lady talk of it yesterday; and of a foolish knight that you brought in one night here to be her wooer.
lines: That quaffing and drinking will undo you: I heard
my lady talk of it yesterday; and of a foolish
knight that you brought in one night here to be her wooer.

speeches.speech: Who, Sir Andrew Aguecheek?
lines: Who, Sir Andrew Aguecheek?

speeches.speech: Ay, he.
lines: Ay, he.

speeches.speech: He's as tall a man as any's in Illyria.
lines: He's as tall a man as any's in Illyria.

speeches.speech: What's that to the purpose?
lines: What's that to the purpose?

speeches.speech: Why, he has three thousand ducats a year.
lines: Why, he has three thousand ducats a year.

speeches.speech: Ay, but he'll have but a year in all these ducats: he's a very fool and a prodigal.
lines: Ay, but he'll have but a year in all these ducats:
he's a very fool and a prodigal.

speeches.speech: Fie, that you'll say so! he plays o' the viol-de-gamboys, and speaks three or four languages word for word without book, and hath all the good gifts of nature.
lines: Fie, that you'll say so! he plays o' the
viol-de-gamboys, and speaks three or four languages
word for word without book, and hath all the good
gifts of nature.

speeches.speech: He hath indeed, almost natural: for besides that he's a fool, he's a great quarreller: and but that he hath the gift of a coward to allay the gust he hath in quarrelling, 'tis thought among the prudent he would quickly have the gift of a grave.
lines: He hath indeed, almost natural: for besides that
he's a fool, he's a great quarreller: and but that
he hath the gift of a coward to allay the gust he
hath in quarrelling, 'tis thought among the prudent
he would quickly have the gift of a grave.

speeches.speech: By this hand, they are scoundrels and subtractors that say so of him. Who are they?
lines: By this hand, they are scoundrels and subtractors
that say so of him. Who are they?

speeches.speech: They that add, moreover, he's drunk nightly in your company.
lines: They that add, moreover, he's drunk nightly in your company.

speeches.speech: With drinking healths to my niece: I'll drink to her as long as there is a passage in my throat and drink in Illyria: he's a coward and a coystrill that will not drink to my niece till his brains turn o' the toe like a parish-top. What, wench! Castiliano vulgo! for here comes Sir Andrew Agueface.
lines: With drinking healths to my niece: I'll drink to
her as long as there is a passage in my throat and
drink in Illyria: he's a coward and a coystrill
that will not drink to my niece till his brains turn
o' the toe like a parish-top. What, wench!
Castiliano vulgo! for here comes Sir Andrew Agueface.

speeches.speech: Sir Toby Belch! how now, Sir Toby Belch!
lines: Sir Toby Belch! how now, Sir Toby Belch!

speeches.speech: Sweet Sir Andrew!
lines: Sweet Sir Andrew!

speeches.speech: Bless you, fair shrew.
lines: Bless you, fair shrew.

speeches.speech: And you too, sir.
lines: And you too, sir.

speeches.speech: Accost, Sir Andrew, accost.
lines: Accost, Sir Andrew, accost.

speeches.speech: What's that?
lines: What's that?

speeches.speech: My niece's chambermaid.
lines: My niece's chambermaid.

speeches.speech: Good Mistress Accost, I desire better acquaintance.
lines: Good Mistress Accost, I desire better acquaintance.

speeches.speech: My name is Mary, sir.
lines: My name is Mary, sir.

speeches.speech: Good Mistress Mary Accost,--
lines: Good Mistress Mary Accost,--

speeches.speech: You mistake, knight; 'accost' is front her, board her, woo her, assail her.
lines: You mistake, knight; 'accost' is front her, board
her, woo her, assail her.

speeches.speech: By my troth, I would not undertake her in this company. Is that the meaning of 'accost'?
lines: By my troth, I would not undertake her in this
company. Is that the meaning of 'accost'?

speeches.speech: Fare you well, gentlemen.
lines: Fare you well, gentlemen.

speeches.speech: An thou let part so, Sir Andrew, would thou mightst never draw sword again.
lines: An thou let part so, Sir Andrew, would thou mightst
never draw sword again.

speeches.speech: An you part so, mistress, I would I might never draw sword again. Fair lady, do you think you have fools in hand?
lines: An you part so, mistress, I would I might never
draw sword again. Fair lady, do you think you have
fools in hand?

speeches.speech: Sir, I have not you by the hand.
lines: Sir, I have not you by the hand.

speeches.speech: Marry, but you shall have; and here's my hand.
lines: Marry, but you shall have; and here's my hand.

speeches.speech: Now, sir, 'thought is free:' I pray you, bring your hand to the buttery-bar and let it drink.
lines: Now, sir, 'thought is free:' I pray you, bring
your hand to the buttery-bar and let it drink.

speeches.speech: Wherefore, sweet-heart? what's your metaphor?
lines: Wherefore, sweet-heart? what's your metaphor?

speeches.speech: It's dry, sir.
lines: It's dry, sir.

speeches.speech: Why, I think so: I am not such an ass but I can keep my hand dry. But what's your jest?
lines: Why, I think so: I am not such an ass but I can
keep my hand dry. But what's your jest?

speeches.speech: A dry jest, sir.
lines: A dry jest, sir.

speeches.speech: Are you full of them?
lines: Are you full of them?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir, I have them at my fingers' ends: marry, now I let go your hand, I am barren.
lines: Ay, sir, I have them at my fingers' ends: marry,
now I let go your hand, I am barren.

speeches.speech: O knight thou lackest a cup of canary: when did I see thee so put down?
lines: O knight thou lackest a cup of canary: when did I
see thee so put down?

speeches.speech: Never in your life, I think; unless you see canary put me down. Methinks sometimes I have no more wit than a Christian or an ordinary man has: but I am a great eater of beef and I believe that does harm to my wit.
lines: Never in your life, I think; unless you see canary
put me down. Methinks sometimes I have no more wit
than a Christian or an ordinary man has: but I am a
great eater of beef and I believe that does harm to my wit.

speeches.speech: No question.
lines: No question.

speeches.speech: An I thought that, I'ld forswear it. I'll ride home to-morrow, Sir Toby.
lines: An I thought that, I'ld forswear it. I'll ride home
to-morrow, Sir Toby.

speeches.speech: Pourquoi, my dear knight?
lines: Pourquoi, my dear knight?

speeches.speech: What is 'Pourquoi'? do or not do? I would I had bestowed that time in the tongues that I have in fencing, dancing and bear-baiting: O, had I but followed the arts!
lines: What is 'Pourquoi'? do or not do? I would I had
bestowed that time in the tongues that I have in
fencing, dancing and bear-baiting: O, had I but
followed the arts!

speeches.speech: Then hadst thou had an excellent head of hair.
lines: Then hadst thou had an excellent head of hair.

speeches.speech: Why, would that have mended my hair?
lines: Why, would that have mended my hair?

speeches.speech: Past question; for thou seest it will not curl by nature.
lines: Past question; for thou seest it will not curl by nature.

speeches.speech: But it becomes me well enough, does't not?
lines: But it becomes me well enough, does't not?

speeches.speech: Excellent; it hangs like flax on a distaff; and I hope to see a housewife take thee between her legs and spin it off.
lines: Excellent; it hangs like flax on a distaff; and I
hope to see a housewife take thee between her legs
and spin it off.

speeches.speech: Faith, I'll home to-morrow, Sir Toby: your niece will not be seen; or if she be, it's four to one she'll none of me: the count himself here hard by woos her.
lines: Faith, I'll home to-morrow, Sir Toby: your niece
will not be seen; or if she be, it's four to one
she'll none of me: the count himself here hard by woos her.

speeches.speech: She'll none o' the count: she'll not match above her degree, neither in estate, years, nor wit; I have heard her swear't. Tut, there's life in't, man.
lines: She'll none o' the count: she'll not match above
her degree, neither in estate, years, nor wit; I
have heard her swear't. Tut, there's life in't,
man.

speeches.speech: I'll stay a month longer. I am a fellow o' the strangest mind i' the world; I delight in masques and revels sometimes altogether.
lines: I'll stay a month longer. I am a fellow o' the
strangest mind i' the world; I delight in masques
and revels sometimes altogether.

speeches.speech: Art thou good at these kickshawses, knight?
lines: Art thou good at these kickshawses, knight?

speeches.speech: As any man in Illyria, whatsoever he be, under the degree of my betters; and yet I will not compare with an old man.
lines: As any man in Illyria, whatsoever he be, under the
degree of my betters; and yet I will not compare
with an old man.

speeches.speech: What is thy excellence in a galliard, knight?
lines: What is thy excellence in a galliard, knight?

speeches.speech: Faith, I can cut a caper.
lines: Faith, I can cut a caper.

speeches.speech: And I can cut the mutton to't.
lines: And I can cut the mutton to't.

speeches.speech: And I think I have the back-trick simply as strong as any man in Illyria.
lines: And I think I have the back-trick simply as strong
as any man in Illyria.

speeches.speech: Wherefore are these things hid? wherefore have these gifts a curtain before 'em? are they like to take dust, like Mistress Mall's picture? why dost thou not go to church in a galliard and come home in a coranto? My very walk should be a jig; I would not so much as make water but in a sink-a-pace. What dost thou mean? Is it a world to hide virtues in? I did think, by the excellent constitution of thy leg, it was formed under the star of a galliard.
lines: Wherefore are these things hid? wherefore have
these gifts a curtain before 'em? are they like to
take dust, like Mistress Mall's picture? why dost
thou not go to church in a galliard and come home in
a coranto? My very walk should be a jig; I would not
so much as make water but in a sink-a-pace. What
dost thou mean? Is it a world to hide virtues in?
I did think, by the excellent constitution of thy
leg, it was formed under the star of a galliard.

speeches.speech: Ay, 'tis strong, and it does indifferent well in a flame-coloured stock. Shall we set about some revels?
lines: Ay, 'tis strong, and it does indifferent well in a
flame-coloured stock. Shall we set about some revels?

speeches.speech: What shall we do else? were we not born under Taurus?
lines: What shall we do else? were we not born under Taurus?

speeches.speech: Taurus! That's sides and heart.
lines: Taurus! That's sides and heart.

speeches.speech: No, sir; it is legs and thighs. Let me see the caper; ha! higher: ha, ha! excellent!
lines: No, sir; it is legs and thighs. Let me see the
caper; ha! higher: ha, ha! excellent!

speeches.speech: If the duke continue these favours towards you, Cesario, you are like to be much advanced: he hath known you but three days, and already you are no stranger.
lines: If the duke continue these favours towards you,
Cesario, you are like to be much advanced: he hath
known you but three days, and already you are no stranger.

speeches.speech: You either fear his humour or my negligence, that you call in question the continuance of his love: is he inconstant, sir, in his favours?
lines: You either fear his humour or my negligence, that
you call in question the continuance of his love:
is he inconstant, sir, in his favours?

speeches.speech: No, believe me.
lines: No, believe me.

speeches.speech: I thank you. Here comes the count.
lines: I thank you. Here comes the count.

speeches.speech: Who saw Cesario, ho?
lines: Who saw Cesario, ho?

speeches.speech: On your attendance, my lord; here.
lines: On your attendance, my lord; here.

speeches.speech: Stand you a while aloof, Cesario, Thou know'st no less but all; I have unclasp'd To thee the book even of my secret soul: Therefore, good youth, address thy gait unto her; Be not denied access, stand at her doors, And tell them, there thy fixed foot shall grow Till thou have audience.
lines: Stand you a while aloof, Cesario,
Thou know'st no less but all; I have unclasp'd
To thee the book even of my secret soul:
Therefore, good youth, address thy gait unto her;
Be not denied access, stand at her doors,
And tell them, there thy fixed foot shall grow
Till thou have audience.

speeches.speech: Sure, my noble lord, If she be so abandon'd to her sorrow As it is spoke, she never will admit me.
lines: Sure, my noble lord,
If she be so abandon'd to her sorrow
As it is spoke, she never will admit me.

speeches.speech: Be clamorous and leap all civil bounds Rather than make unprofited return.
lines: Be clamorous and leap all civil bounds
Rather than make unprofited return.

speeches.speech: Say I do speak with her, my lord, what then?
lines: Say I do speak with her, my lord, what then?

speeches.speech: O, then unfold the passion of my love, Surprise her with discourse of my dear faith: It shall become thee well to act my woes; She will attend it better in thy youth Than in a nuncio's of more grave aspect.
lines: O, then unfold the passion of my love,
Surprise her with discourse of my dear faith:
It shall become thee well to act my woes;
She will attend it better in thy youth
Than in a nuncio's of more grave aspect.

speeches.speech: I think not so, my lord.
lines: I think not so, my lord.

speeches.speech: Dear lad, believe it; For they shall yet belie thy happy years, That say thou art a man: Diana's lip Is not more smooth and rubious; thy small pipe Is as the maiden's organ, shrill and sound, And all is semblative a woman's part. I know thy constellation is right apt For this affair. Some four or five attend him; All, if you will; for I myself am best When least in company. Prosper well in this, And thou shalt live as freely as thy lord, To call his fortunes thine.
lines: Dear lad, believe it;
For they shall yet belie thy happy years,
That say thou art a man: Diana's lip
Is not more smooth and rubious; thy small pipe
Is as the maiden's organ, shrill and sound,
And all is semblative a woman's part.
I know thy constellation is right apt
For this affair. Some four or five attend him;
All, if you will; for I myself am best
When least in company. Prosper well in this,
And thou shalt live as freely as thy lord,
To call his fortunes thine.

speeches.speech: I'll do my best To woo your lady: [Aside] yet, a barful strife! Whoe'er I woo, myself would be his wife.
lines: I'll do my best
To woo your lady:
[Aside]
yet, a barful strife!
Whoe'er I woo, myself would be his wife.

speeches.speech: Nay, either tell me where thou hast been, or I will not open my lips so wide as a bristle may enter in way of thy excuse: my lady will hang thee for thy absence.
lines: Nay, either tell me where thou hast been, or I will
not open my lips so wide as a bristle may enter in
way of thy excuse: my lady will hang thee for thy absence.

speeches.speech: Let her hang me: he that is well hanged in this world needs to fear no colours.
lines: Let her hang me: he that is well hanged in this
world needs to fear no colours.

speeches.speech: Make that good.
lines: Make that good.

speeches.speech: He shall see none to fear.
lines: He shall see none to fear.

speeches.speech: A good lenten answer: I can tell thee where that saying was born, of 'I fear no colours.'
lines: A good lenten answer: I can tell thee where that
saying was born, of 'I fear no colours.'

speeches.speech: Where, good Mistress Mary?
lines: Where, good Mistress Mary?

speeches.speech: In the wars; and that may you be bold to say in your foolery.
lines: In the wars; and that may you be bold to say in your foolery.

speeches.speech: Well, God give them wisdom that have it; and those that are fools, let them use their talents.
lines: Well, God give them wisdom that have it; and those
that are fools, let them use their talents.

speeches.speech: Yet you will be hanged for being so long absent; or, to be turned away, is not that as good as a hanging to you?
lines: Yet you will be hanged for being so long absent; or,
to be turned away, is not that as good as a hanging to you?

speeches.speech: Many a good hanging prevents a bad marriage; and, for turning away, let summer bear it out.
lines: Many a good hanging prevents a bad marriage; and,
for turning away, let summer bear it out.

speeches.speech: You are resolute, then?
lines: You are resolute, then?

speeches.speech: Not so, neither; but I am resolved on two points.
lines: Not so, neither; but I am resolved on two points.

speeches.speech: That if one break, the other will hold; or, if both break, your gaskins fall.
lines: That if one break, the other will hold; or, if both
break, your gaskins fall.

speeches.speech: Apt, in good faith; very apt. Well, go thy way; if Sir Toby would leave drinking, thou wert as witty a piece of Eve's flesh as any in Illyria.
lines: Apt, in good faith; very apt. Well, go thy way; if
Sir Toby would leave drinking, thou wert as witty a
piece of Eve's flesh as any in Illyria.

speeches.speech: Peace, you rogue, no more o' that. Here comes my lady: make your excuse wisely, you were best.
lines: Peace, you rogue, no more o' that. Here comes my
lady: make your excuse wisely, you were best.

speeches.speech: Wit, an't be thy will, put me into good fooling! Those wits, that think they have thee, do very oft prove fools; and I, that am sure I lack thee, may pass for a wise man: for what says Quinapalus? 'Better a witty fool, than a foolish wit.' [Enter OLIVIA with MALVOLIO] God bless thee, lady!
lines: Wit, an't be thy will, put me into good fooling!
Those wits, that think they have thee, do very oft
prove fools; and I, that am sure I lack thee, may
pass for a wise man: for what says Quinapalus?
'Better a witty fool, than a foolish wit.'
[Enter OLIVIA with MALVOLIO]
God bless thee, lady!

speeches.speech: Take the fool away.
lines: Take the fool away.

speeches.speech: Do you not hear, fellows? Take away the lady.
lines: Do you not hear, fellows? Take away the lady.

speeches.speech: Go to, you're a dry fool; I'll no more of you: besides, you grow dishonest.
lines: Go to, you're a dry fool; I'll no more of you:
besides, you grow dishonest.

speeches.speech: Two faults, madonna, that drink and good counsel will amend: for give the dry fool drink, then is the fool not dry: bid the dishonest man mend himself; if he mend, he is no longer dishonest; if he cannot, let the botcher mend him. Any thing that's mended is but patched: virtue that transgresses is but patched with sin; and sin that amends is but patched with virtue. If that this simple syllogism will serve, so; if it will not, what remedy? As there is no true cuckold but calamity, so beauty's a flower. The lady bade take away the fool; therefore, I say again, take her away.
lines: Two faults, madonna, that drink and good counsel
will amend: for give the dry fool drink, then is
the fool not dry: bid the dishonest man mend
himself; if he mend, he is no longer dishonest; if
he cannot, let the botcher mend him. Any thing
that's mended is but patched: virtue that
transgresses is but patched with sin; and sin that
amends is but patched with virtue. If that this
simple syllogism will serve, so; if it will not,
what remedy? As there is no true cuckold but
calamity, so beauty's a flower. The lady bade take
away the fool; therefore, I say again, take her away.

speeches.speech: Sir, I bade them take away you.
lines: Sir, I bade them take away you.

speeches.speech: Misprision in the highest degree! Lady, cucullus non facit monachum; that's as much to say as I wear not motley in my brain. Good madonna, give me leave to prove you a fool.
lines: Misprision in the highest degree! Lady, cucullus non
facit monachum; that's as much to say as I wear not
motley in my brain. Good madonna, give me leave to
prove you a fool.

speeches.speech: Can you do it?
lines: Can you do it?

speeches.speech: Dexterously, good madonna.
lines: Dexterously, good madonna.

speeches.speech: Make your proof.
lines: Make your proof.

speeches.speech: I must catechise you for it, madonna: good my mouse of virtue, answer me.
lines: I must catechise you for it, madonna: good my mouse
of virtue, answer me.

speeches.speech: Well, sir, for want of other idleness, I'll bide your proof.
lines: Well, sir, for want of other idleness, I'll bide your proof.

speeches.speech: Good madonna, why mournest thou?
lines: Good madonna, why mournest thou?

speeches.speech: Good fool, for my brother's death.
lines: Good fool, for my brother's death.

speeches.speech: I think his soul is in hell, madonna.
lines: I think his soul is in hell, madonna.

speeches.speech: I know his soul is in heaven, fool.
lines: I know his soul is in heaven, fool.

speeches.speech: The more fool, madonna, to mourn for your brother's soul being in heaven. Take away the fool, gentlemen.
lines: The more fool, madonna, to mourn for your brother's
soul being in heaven. Take away the fool, gentlemen.

speeches.speech: What think you of this fool, Malvolio? doth he not mend?
lines: What think you of this fool, Malvolio? doth he not mend?

speeches.speech: Yes, and shall do till the pangs of death shake him: infirmity, that decays the wise, doth ever make the better fool.
lines: Yes, and shall do till the pangs of death shake him:
infirmity, that decays the wise, doth ever make the
better fool.

speeches.speech: God send you, sir, a speedy infirmity, for the better increasing your folly! Sir Toby will be sworn that I am no fox; but he will not pass his word for two pence that you are no fool.
lines: God send you, sir, a speedy infirmity, for the
better increasing your folly! Sir Toby will be
sworn that I am no fox; but he will not pass his
word for two pence that you are no fool.

speeches.speech: How say you to that, Malvolio?
lines: How say you to that, Malvolio?

speeches.speech: I marvel your ladyship takes delight in such a barren rascal: I saw him put down the other day with an ordinary fool that has no more brain than a stone. Look you now, he's out of his guard already; unless you laugh and minister occasion to him, he is gagged. I protest, I take these wise men, that crow so at these set kind of fools, no better than the fools' zanies.
lines: I marvel your ladyship takes delight in such a
barren rascal: I saw him put down the other day
with an ordinary fool that has no more brain
than a stone. Look you now, he's out of his guard
already; unless you laugh and minister occasion to
him, he is gagged. I protest, I take these wise men,
that crow so at these set kind of fools, no better
than the fools' zanies.

speeches.speech: Oh, you are sick of self-love, Malvolio, and taste with a distempered appetite. To be generous, guiltless and of free disposition, is to take those things for bird-bolts that you deem cannon-bullets: there is no slander in an allowed fool, though he do nothing but rail; nor no railing in a known discreet man, though he do nothing but reprove.
lines: Oh, you are sick of self-love, Malvolio, and taste
with a distempered appetite. To be generous,
guiltless and of free disposition, is to take those
things for bird-bolts that you deem cannon-bullets:
there is no slander in an allowed fool, though he do
nothing but rail; nor no railing in a known discreet
man, though he do nothing but reprove.

speeches.speech: Now Mercury endue thee with leasing, for thou speakest well of fools!
lines: Now Mercury endue thee with leasing, for thou
speakest well of fools!

speeches.speech: Madam, there is at the gate a young gentleman much desires to speak with you.
lines: Madam, there is at the gate a young gentleman much
desires to speak with you.

speeches.speech: From the Count Orsino, is it?
lines: From the Count Orsino, is it?

speeches.speech: I know not, madam: 'tis a fair young man, and well attended.
lines: I know not, madam: 'tis a fair young man, and well attended.

speeches.speech: Who of my people hold him in delay?
lines: Who of my people hold him in delay?

speeches.speech: Sir Toby, madam, your kinsman.
lines: Sir Toby, madam, your kinsman.

speeches.speech: Fetch him off, I pray you; he speaks nothing but madman: fie on him! [Exit MARIA] Go you, Malvolio: if it be a suit from the count, I am sick, or not at home; what you will, to dismiss it. [Exit MALVOLIO] Now you see, sir, how your fooling grows old, and people dislike it.
lines: Fetch him off, I pray you; he speaks nothing but
madman: fie on him!
[Exit MARIA]
Go you, Malvolio: if it be a suit from the count, I
am sick, or not at home; what you will, to dismiss it.
[Exit MALVOLIO]
Now you see, sir, how your fooling grows old, and
people dislike it.

speeches.speech: Thou hast spoke for us, madonna, as if thy eldest son should be a fool; whose skull Jove cram with brains! for,--here he comes,--one of thy kin has a most weak pia mater.
lines: Thou hast spoke for us, madonna, as if thy eldest
son should be a fool; whose skull Jove cram with
brains! for,--here he comes,--one of thy kin has a
most weak pia mater.

speeches.speech: By mine honour, half drunk. What is he at the gate, cousin?
lines: By mine honour, half drunk. What is he at the gate, cousin?

speeches.speech: A gentleman.
lines: A gentleman.

speeches.speech: A gentleman! what gentleman?
lines: A gentleman! what gentleman?

speeches.speech: 'Tis a gentle man here--a plague o' these pickle-herring! How now, sot!
lines: 'Tis a gentle man here--a plague o' these
pickle-herring! How now, sot!

speeches.speech: Good Sir Toby!
lines: Good Sir Toby!

speeches.speech: Cousin, cousin, how have you come so early by this lethargy?
lines: Cousin, cousin, how have you come so early by this lethargy?

speeches.speech: Lechery! I defy lechery. There's one at the gate.
lines: Lechery! I defy lechery. There's one at the gate.

speeches.speech: Ay, marry, what is he?
lines: Ay, marry, what is he?

speeches.speech: Let him be the devil, an he will, I care not: give me faith, say I. Well, it's all one.
lines: Let him be the devil, an he will, I care not: give
me faith, say I. Well, it's all one.

speeches.speech: What's a drunken man like, fool?
lines: What's a drunken man like, fool?

speeches.speech: Like a drowned man, a fool and a mad man: one draught above heat makes him a fool; the second mads him; and a third drowns him.
lines: Like a drowned man, a fool and a mad man: one
draught above heat makes him a fool; the second mads
him; and a third drowns him.

speeches.speech: Go thou and seek the crowner, and let him sit o' my coz; for he's in the third degree of drink, he's drowned: go, look after him.
lines: Go thou and seek the crowner, and let him sit o' my
coz; for he's in the third degree of drink, he's
drowned: go, look after him.

speeches.speech: He is but mad yet, madonna; and the fool shall look to the madman.
lines: He is but mad yet, madonna; and the fool shall look
to the madman.

speeches.speech: Madam, yond young fellow swears he will speak with you. I told him you were sick; he takes on him to understand so much, and therefore comes to speak with you. I told him you were asleep; he seems to have a foreknowledge of that too, and therefore comes to speak with you. What is to be said to him, lady? he's fortified against any denial.
lines: Madam, yond young fellow swears he will speak with
you. I told him you were sick; he takes on him to
understand so much, and therefore comes to speak
with you. I told him you were asleep; he seems to
have a foreknowledge of that too, and therefore
comes to speak with you. What is to be said to him,
lady? he's fortified against any denial.

speeches.speech: Tell him he shall not speak with me.
lines: Tell him he shall not speak with me.

speeches.speech: Has been told so; and he says, he'll stand at your door like a sheriff's post, and be the supporter to a bench, but he'll speak with you.
lines: Has been told so; and he says, he'll stand at your
door like a sheriff's post, and be the supporter to
a bench, but he'll speak with you.

speeches.speech: What kind o' man is he?
lines: What kind o' man is he?

speeches.speech: Why, of mankind.
lines: Why, of mankind.

speeches.speech: What manner of man?
lines: What manner of man?

speeches.speech: Of very ill manner; he'll speak with you, will you or no.
lines: Of very ill manner; he'll speak with you, will you or no.

speeches.speech: Of what personage and years is he?
lines: Of what personage and years is he?

speeches.speech: Not yet old enough for a man, nor young enough for a boy; as a squash is before 'tis a peascod, or a cooling when 'tis almost an apple: 'tis with him in standing water, between boy and man. He is very well-favoured and he speaks very shrewishly; one would think his mother's milk were scarce out of him.
lines: Not yet old enough for a man, nor young enough for
a boy; as a squash is before 'tis a peascod, or a
cooling when 'tis almost an apple: 'tis with him
in standing water, between boy and man. He is very
well-favoured and he speaks very shrewishly; one
would think his mother's milk were scarce out of him.

speeches.speech: Let him approach: call in my gentlewoman.
lines: Let him approach: call in my gentlewoman.

speeches.speech: Gentlewoman, my lady calls.
lines: Gentlewoman, my lady calls.

speeches.speech: Give me my veil: come, throw it o'er my face. We'll once more hear Orsino's embassy.
lines: Give me my veil: come, throw it o'er my face.
We'll once more hear Orsino's embassy.

speeches.speech: The honourable lady of the house, which is she?
lines: The honourable lady of the house, which is she?

speeches.speech: Speak to me; I shall answer for her. Your will?
lines: Speak to me; I shall answer for her.
Your will?

speeches.speech: Most radiant, exquisite and unmatchable beauty,--I pray you, tell me if this be the lady of the house, for I never saw her: I would be loath to cast away my speech, for besides that it is excellently well penned, I have taken great pains to con it. Good beauties, let me sustain no scorn; I am very comptible, even to the least sinister usage.
lines: Most radiant, exquisite and unmatchable beauty,--I
pray you, tell me if this be the lady of the house,
for I never saw her: I would be loath to cast away
my speech, for besides that it is excellently well
penned, I have taken great pains to con it. Good
beauties, let me sustain no scorn; I am very
comptible, even to the least sinister usage.

speeches.speech: Whence came you, sir?
lines: Whence came you, sir?

speeches.speech: I can say little more than I have studied, and that question's out of my part. Good gentle one, give me modest assurance if you be the lady of the house, that I may proceed in my speech.
lines: I can say little more than I have studied, and that
question's out of my part. Good gentle one, give me
modest assurance if you be the lady of the house,
that I may proceed in my speech.

speeches.speech: Are you a comedian?
lines: Are you a comedian?

speeches.speech: No, my profound heart: and yet, by the very fangs of malice I swear, I am not that I play. Are you the lady of the house?
lines: No, my profound heart: and yet, by the very fangs
of malice I swear, I am not that I play. Are you
the lady of the house?

speeches.speech: If I do not usurp myself, I am.
lines: If I do not usurp myself, I am.

speeches.speech: Most certain, if you are she, you do usurp yourself; for what is yours to bestow is not yours to reserve. But this is from my commission: I will on with my speech in your praise, and then show you the heart of my message.
lines: Most certain, if you are she, you do usurp
yourself; for what is yours to bestow is not yours
to reserve. But this is from my commission: I will
on with my speech in your praise, and then show you
the heart of my message.

speeches.speech: Come to what is important in't: I forgive you the praise.
lines: Come to what is important in't: I forgive you the praise.

speeches.speech: Alas, I took great pains to study it, and 'tis poetical.
lines: Alas, I took great pains to study it, and 'tis poetical.

speeches.speech: It is the more like to be feigned: I pray you, keep it in. I heard you were saucy at my gates, and allowed your approach rather to wonder at you than to hear you. If you be not mad, be gone; if you have reason, be brief: 'tis not that time of moon with me to make one in so skipping a dialogue.
lines: It is the more like to be feigned: I pray you,
keep it in. I heard you were saucy at my gates,
and allowed your approach rather to wonder at you
than to hear you. If you be not mad, be gone; if
you have reason, be brief: 'tis not that time of
moon with me to make one in so skipping a dialogue.

speeches.speech: Will you hoist sail, sir? here lies your way.
lines: Will you hoist sail, sir? here lies your way.

speeches.speech: No, good swabber; I am to hull here a little longer. Some mollification for your giant, sweet lady. Tell me your mind: I am a messenger.
lines: No, good swabber; I am to hull here a little
longer. Some mollification for your giant, sweet
lady. Tell me your mind: I am a messenger.

speeches.speech: Sure, you have some hideous matter to deliver, when the courtesy of it is so fearful. Speak your office.
lines: Sure, you have some hideous matter to deliver, when
the courtesy of it is so fearful. Speak your office.

speeches.speech: It alone concerns your ear. I bring no overture of war, no taxation of homage: I hold the olive in my hand; my words are as fun of peace as matter.
lines: It alone concerns your ear. I bring no overture of
war, no taxation of homage: I hold the olive in my
hand; my words are as fun of peace as matter.

speeches.speech: Yet you began rudely. What are you? what would you?
lines: Yet you began rudely. What are you? what would you?

speeches.speech: The rudeness that hath appeared in me have I learned from my entertainment. What I am, and what I would, are as secret as maidenhead; to your ears, divinity, to any other's, profanation.
lines: The rudeness that hath appeared in me have I
learned from my entertainment. What I am, and what I
would, are as secret as maidenhead; to your ears,
divinity, to any other's, profanation.

speeches.speech: Give us the place alone: we will hear this divinity. [Exeunt MARIA and Attendants] Now, sir, what is your text?
lines: Give us the place alone: we will hear this divinity.
[Exeunt MARIA and Attendants]
Now, sir, what is your text?

speeches.speech: Most sweet lady,--
lines: Most sweet lady,--

speeches.speech: A comfortable doctrine, and much may be said of it. Where lies your text?
lines: A comfortable doctrine, and much may be said of it.
Where lies your text?

speeches.speech: In Orsino's bosom.
lines: In Orsino's bosom.

speeches.speech: In his bosom! In what chapter of his bosom?
lines: In his bosom! In what chapter of his bosom?

speeches.speech: To answer by the method, in the first of his heart.
lines: To answer by the method, in the first of his heart.

speeches.speech: O, I have read it: it is heresy. Have you no more to say?
lines: O, I have read it: it is heresy. Have you no more to say?

speeches.speech: Good madam, let me see your face.
lines: Good madam, let me see your face.

speeches.speech: Have you any commission from your lord to negotiate with my face? You are now out of your text: but we will draw the curtain and show you the picture. Look you, sir, such a one I was this present: is't not well done?
lines: Have you any commission from your lord to negotiate
with my face? You are now out of your text: but
we will draw the curtain and show you the picture.
Look you, sir, such a one I was this present: is't
not well done?

speeches.speech: Excellently done, if God did all.
lines: Excellently done, if God did all.

speeches.speech: 'Tis in grain, sir; 'twill endure wind and weather.
lines: 'Tis in grain, sir; 'twill endure wind and weather.

speeches.speech: 'Tis beauty truly blent, whose red and white Nature's own sweet and cunning hand laid on: Lady, you are the cruell'st she alive, If you will lead these graces to the grave And leave the world no copy.
lines: 'Tis beauty truly blent, whose red and white
Nature's own sweet and cunning hand laid on:
Lady, you are the cruell'st she alive,
If you will lead these graces to the grave
And leave the world no copy.

speeches.speech: O, sir, I will not be so hard-hearted; I will give out divers schedules of my beauty: it shall be inventoried, and every particle and utensil labelled to my will: as, item, two lips, indifferent red; item, two grey eyes, with lids to them; item, one neck, one chin, and so forth. Were you sent hither to praise me?
lines: O, sir, I will not be so hard-hearted; I will give
out divers schedules of my beauty: it shall be
inventoried, and every particle and utensil
labelled to my will: as, item, two lips,
indifferent red; item, two grey eyes, with lids to
them; item, one neck, one chin, and so forth. Were
you sent hither to praise me?

speeches.speech: I see you what you are, you are too proud; But, if you were the devil, you are fair. My lord and master loves you: O, such love Could be but recompensed, though you were crown'd The nonpareil of beauty!
lines: I see you what you are, you are too proud;
But, if you were the devil, you are fair.
My lord and master loves you: O, such love
Could be but recompensed, though you were crown'd
The nonpareil of beauty!

speeches.speech: How does he love me?
lines: How does he love me?

speeches.speech: With adorations, fertile tears, With groans that thunder love, with sighs of fire.
lines: With adorations, fertile tears,
With groans that thunder love, with sighs of fire.

speeches.speech: Your lord does know my mind; I cannot love him: Yet I suppose him virtuous, know him noble, Of great estate, of fresh and stainless youth; In voices well divulged, free, learn'd and valiant; And in dimension and the shape of nature A gracious person: but yet I cannot love him; He might have took his answer long ago.
lines: Your lord does know my mind; I cannot love him:
Yet I suppose him virtuous, know him noble,
Of great estate, of fresh and stainless youth;
In voices well divulged, free, learn'd and valiant;
And in dimension and the shape of nature
A gracious person: but yet I cannot love him;
He might have took his answer long ago.

speeches.speech: If I did love you in my master's flame, With such a suffering, such a deadly life, In your denial I would find no sense; I would not understand it.
lines: If I did love you in my master's flame,
With such a suffering, such a deadly life,
In your denial I would find no sense;
I would not understand it.

speeches.speech: Why, what would you?
lines: Why, what would you?

speeches.speech: Make me a willow cabin at your gate, And call upon my soul within the house; Write loyal cantons of contemned love And sing them loud even in the dead of night; Halloo your name to the reverberate hills And make the babbling gossip of the air Cry out 'Olivia!' O, You should not rest Between the elements of air and earth, But you should pity me!
lines: Make me a willow cabin at your gate,
And call upon my soul within the house;
Write loyal cantons of contemned love
And sing them loud even in the dead of night;
Halloo your name to the reverberate hills
And make the babbling gossip of the air
Cry out 'Olivia!' O, You should not rest
Between the elements of air and earth,
But you should pity me!

speeches.speech: You might do much. What is your parentage?
lines: You might do much.
What is your parentage?

speeches.speech: Above my fortunes, yet my state is well: I am a gentleman.
lines: Above my fortunes, yet my state is well:
I am a gentleman.

speeches.speech: Get you to your lord; I cannot love him: let him send no more; Unless, perchance, you come to me again, To tell me how he takes it. Fare you well: I thank you for your pains: spend this for me.
lines: Get you to your lord;
I cannot love him: let him send no more;
Unless, perchance, you come to me again,
To tell me how he takes it. Fare you well:
I thank you for your pains: spend this for me.

speeches.speech: I am no fee'd post, lady; keep your purse: My master, not myself, lacks recompense. Love make his heart of flint that you shall love; And let your fervor, like my master's, be Placed in contempt! Farewell, fair cruelty.
lines: I am no fee'd post, lady; keep your purse:
My master, not myself, lacks recompense.
Love make his heart of flint that you shall love;
And let your fervor, like my master's, be
Placed in contempt! Farewell, fair cruelty.

speeches.speech: 'What is your parentage?' 'Above my fortunes, yet my state is well: I am a gentleman.' I'll be sworn thou art; Thy tongue, thy face, thy limbs, actions and spirit, Do give thee five-fold blazon: not too fast: soft, soft! Unless the master were the man. How now! Even so quickly may one catch the plague? Methinks I feel this youth's perfections With an invisible and subtle stealth To creep in at mine eyes. Well, let it be. What ho, Malvolio!
lines: 'What is your parentage?'
'Above my fortunes, yet my state is well:
I am a gentleman.' I'll be sworn thou art;
Thy tongue, thy face, thy limbs, actions and spirit,
Do give thee five-fold blazon: not too fast:
soft, soft!
Unless the master were the man. How now!
Even so quickly may one catch the plague?
Methinks I feel this youth's perfections
With an invisible and subtle stealth
To creep in at mine eyes. Well, let it be.
What ho, Malvolio!

speeches.speech: Here, madam, at your service.
lines: Here, madam, at your service.

speeches.speech: Run after that same peevish messenger, The county's man: he left this ring behind him, Would I or not: tell him I'll none of it. Desire him not to flatter with his lord, Nor hold him up with hopes; I am not for him: If that the youth will come this way to-morrow, I'll give him reasons for't: hie thee, Malvolio.
lines: Run after that same peevish messenger,
The county's man: he left this ring behind him,
Would I or not: tell him I'll none of it.
Desire him not to flatter with his lord,
Nor hold him up with hopes; I am not for him:
If that the youth will come this way to-morrow,
I'll give him reasons for't: hie thee, Malvolio.

speeches.speech: Madam, I will.
lines: Madam, I will.

speeches.speech: I do I know not what, and fear to find Mine eye too great a flatterer for my mind. Fate, show thy force: ourselves we do not owe; What is decreed must be, and be this so.
lines: I do I know not what, and fear to find
Mine eye too great a flatterer for my mind.
Fate, show thy force: ourselves we do not owe;
What is decreed must be, and be this so.

speeches.speech: Will you stay no longer? nor will you not that I go with you?
lines: Will you stay no longer? nor will you not that I go with you?

speeches.speech: By your patience, no. My stars shine darkly over me: the malignancy of my fate might perhaps distemper yours; therefore I shall crave of you your leave that I may bear my evils alone: it were a bad recompense for your love, to lay any of them on you.
lines: By your patience, no. My stars shine darkly over
me: the malignancy of my fate might perhaps
distemper yours; therefore I shall crave of you your
leave that I may bear my evils alone: it were a bad
recompense for your love, to lay any of them on you.

speeches.speech: No, sooth, sir: my determinate voyage is mere extravagancy. But I perceive in you so excellent a touch of modesty, that you will not extort from me what I am willing to keep in; therefore it charges me in manners the rather to express myself. You must know of me then, Antonio, my name is Sebastian, which I called Roderigo. My father was that Sebastian of Messaline, whom I know you have heard of. He left behind him myself and a sister, both born in an hour: if the heavens had been pleased, would we had so ended! but you, sir, altered that; for some hour before you took me from the breach of the sea was my sister drowned.
lines: No, sooth, sir: my determinate voyage is mere
extravagancy. But I perceive in you so excellent a
touch of modesty, that you will not extort from me
what I am willing to keep in; therefore it charges
me in manners the rather to express myself. You
must know of me then, Antonio, my name is Sebastian,
which I called Roderigo. My father was that
Sebastian of Messaline, whom I know you have heard
of. He left behind him myself and a sister, both
born in an hour: if the heavens had been pleased,
would we had so ended! but you, sir, altered that;
for some hour before you took me from the breach of
the sea was my sister drowned.

speeches.speech: Alas the day!
lines: Alas the day!

speeches.speech: A lady, sir, though it was said she much resembled me, was yet of many accounted beautiful: but, though I could not with such estimable wonder overfar believe that, yet thus far I will boldly publish her; she bore a mind that envy could not but call fair. She is drowned already, sir, with salt water, though I seem to drown her remembrance again with more.
lines: A lady, sir, though it was said she much resembled
me, was yet of many accounted beautiful: but,
though I could not with such estimable wonder
overfar believe that, yet thus far I will boldly
publish her; she bore a mind that envy could not but
call fair. She is drowned already, sir, with salt
water, though I seem to drown her remembrance again with more.

speeches.speech: Pardon me, sir, your bad entertainment.
lines: Pardon me, sir, your bad entertainment.

speeches.speech: O good Antonio, forgive me your trouble.
lines: O good Antonio, forgive me your trouble.

speeches.speech: If you will not murder me for my love, let me be your servant.
lines: If you will not murder me for my love, let me be
your servant.

speeches.speech: If you will not undo what you have done, that is, kill him whom you have recovered, desire it not. Fare ye well at once: my bosom is full of kindness, and I am yet so near the manners of my mother, that upon the least occasion more mine eyes will tell tales of me. I am bound to the Count Orsino's court: farewell.
lines: If you will not undo what you have done, that is,
kill him whom you have recovered, desire it not.
Fare ye well at once: my bosom is full of kindness,
and I am yet so near the manners of my mother, that
upon the least occasion more mine eyes will tell
tales of me. I am bound to the Count Orsino's court: farewell.

speeches.speech: The gentleness of all the gods go with thee! I have many enemies in Orsino's court, Else would I very shortly see thee there. But, come what may, I do adore thee so, That danger shall seem sport, and I will go.
lines: The gentleness of all the gods go with thee!
I have many enemies in Orsino's court,
Else would I very shortly see thee there.
But, come what may, I do adore thee so,
That danger shall seem sport, and I will go.

speeches.speech: Were not you even now with the Countess Olivia?
lines: Were not you even now with the Countess Olivia?

speeches.speech: Even now, sir; on a moderate pace I have since arrived but hither.
lines: Even now, sir; on a moderate pace I have since
arrived but hither.

speeches.speech: She returns this ring to you, sir: you might have saved me my pains, to have taken it away yourself. She adds, moreover, that you should put your lord into a desperate assurance she will none of him: and one thing more, that you be never so hardy to come again in his affairs, unless it be to report your lord's taking of this. Receive it so.
lines: She returns this ring to you, sir: you might have
saved me my pains, to have taken it away yourself.
She adds, moreover, that you should put your lord
into a desperate assurance she will none of him:
and one thing more, that you be never so hardy to
come again in his affairs, unless it be to report
your lord's taking of this. Receive it so.

speeches.speech: She took the ring of me: I'll none of it.
lines: She took the ring of me: I'll none of it.

speeches.speech: Come, sir, you peevishly threw it to her; and her will is, it should be so returned: if it be worth stooping for, there it lies in your eye; if not, be it his that finds it.
lines: Come, sir, you peevishly threw it to her; and her
will is, it should be so returned: if it be worth
stooping for, there it lies in your eye; if not, be
it his that finds it.

speeches.speech: I left no ring with her: what means this lady? Fortune forbid my outside have not charm'd her! She made good view of me; indeed, so much, That sure methought her eyes had lost her tongue, For she did speak in starts distractedly. She loves me, sure; the cunning of her passion Invites me in this churlish messenger. None of my lord's ring! why, he sent her none. I am the man: if it be so, as 'tis, Poor lady, she were better love a dream. Disguise, I see, thou art a wickedness, Wherein the pregnant enemy does much. How easy is it for the proper-false In women's waxen hearts to set their forms! Alas, our frailty is the cause, not we! For such as we are made of, such we be. How will this fadge? my master loves her dearly; And I, poor monster, fond as much on him; And she, mistaken, seems to dote on me. What will become of this? As I am man, My state is desperate for my master's love; As I am woman,--now alas the day!-- What thriftless sighs shall poor Olivia breathe! O time! thou must untangle this, not I; It is too hard a knot for me to untie!
lines: I left no ring with her: what means this lady?
Fortune forbid my outside have not charm'd her!
She made good view of me; indeed, so much,
That sure methought her eyes had lost her tongue,
For she did speak in starts distractedly.
She loves me, sure; the cunning of her passion
Invites me in this churlish messenger.
None of my lord's ring! why, he sent her none.
I am the man: if it be so, as 'tis,
Poor lady, she were better love a dream.
Disguise, I see, thou art a wickedness,
Wherein the pregnant enemy does much.
How easy is it for the proper-false
In women's waxen hearts to set their forms!
Alas, our frailty is the cause, not we!
For such as we are made of, such we be.
How will this fadge? my master loves her dearly;
And I, poor monster, fond as much on him;
And she, mistaken, seems to dote on me.
What will become of this? As I am man,
My state is desperate for my master's love;
As I am woman,--now alas the day!--
What thriftless sighs shall poor Olivia breathe!
O time! thou must untangle this, not I;
It is too hard a knot for me to untie!

speeches.speech: Approach, Sir Andrew: not to be abed after midnight is to be up betimes; and 'diluculo surgere,' thou know'st,--
lines: Approach, Sir Andrew: not to be abed after
midnight is to be up betimes; and 'diluculo
surgere,' thou know'st,--

speeches.speech: Nay, my troth, I know not: but I know, to be up late is to be up late.
lines: Nay, my troth, I know not: but I know, to be up
late is to be up late.

speeches.speech: A false conclusion: I hate it as an unfilled can. To be up after midnight and to go to bed then, is early: so that to go to bed after midnight is to go to bed betimes. Does not our life consist of the four elements?
lines: A false conclusion: I hate it as an unfilled can.
To be up after midnight and to go to bed then, is
early: so that to go to bed after midnight is to go
to bed betimes. Does not our life consist of the
four elements?

speeches.speech: Faith, so they say; but I think it rather consists of eating and drinking.
lines: Faith, so they say; but I think it rather consists
of eating and drinking.

speeches.speech: Thou'rt a scholar; let us therefore eat and drink. Marian, I say! a stoup of wine!
lines: Thou'rt a scholar; let us therefore eat and drink.
Marian, I say! a stoup of wine!

speeches.speech: Here comes the fool, i' faith.
lines: Here comes the fool, i' faith.

speeches.speech: How now, my hearts! did you never see the picture of 'we three'?
lines: How now, my hearts! did you never see the picture
of 'we three'?

speeches.speech: Welcome, ass. Now let's have a catch.
lines: Welcome, ass. Now let's have a catch.

speeches.speech: By my troth, the fool has an excellent breast. I had rather than forty shillings I had such a leg, and so sweet a breath to sing, as the fool has. In sooth, thou wast in very gracious fooling last night, when thou spokest of Pigrogromitus, of the Vapians passing the equinoctial of Queubus: 'twas very good, i' faith. I sent thee sixpence for thy leman: hadst it?
lines: By my troth, the fool has an excellent breast. I
had rather than forty shillings I had such a leg,
and so sweet a breath to sing, as the fool has. In
sooth, thou wast in very gracious fooling last
night, when thou spokest of Pigrogromitus, of the
Vapians passing the equinoctial of Queubus: 'twas
very good, i' faith. I sent thee sixpence for thy
leman: hadst it?

speeches.speech: I did impeticos thy gratillity; for Malvolio's nose is no whipstock: my lady has a white hand, and the Myrmidons are no bottle-ale houses.
lines: I did impeticos thy gratillity; for Malvolio's nose
is no whipstock: my lady has a white hand, and the
Myrmidons are no bottle-ale houses.

speeches.speech: Excellent! why, this is the best fooling, when all is done. Now, a song.
lines: Excellent! why, this is the best fooling, when all
is done. Now, a song.

speeches.speech: Come on; there is sixpence for you: let's have a song.
lines: Come on; there is sixpence for you: let's have a song.

speeches.speech: There's a testril of me too: if one knight give a--
lines: There's a testril of me too: if one knight give a--

speeches.speech: Would you have a love-song, or a song of good life?
lines: Would you have a love-song, or a song of good life?

speeches.speech: A love-song, a love-song.
lines: A love-song, a love-song.

speeches.speech: Ay, ay: I care not for good life.
lines: Ay, ay: I care not for good life.

speeches.speech: [Sings] O mistress mine, where are you roaming? O, stay and hear; your true love's coming, That can sing both high and low: Trip no further, pretty sweeting; Journeys end in lovers meeting, Every wise man's son doth know.
lines: [Sings]
O mistress mine, where are you roaming?
O, stay and hear; your true love's coming,
That can sing both high and low:
Trip no further, pretty sweeting;
Journeys end in lovers meeting,
Every wise man's son doth know.

speeches.speech: Excellent good, i' faith.
lines: Excellent good, i' faith.

speeches.speech: Good, good.
lines: Good, good.

speeches.speech: [Sings] What is love? 'tis not hereafter; Present mirth hath present laughter; What's to come is still unsure: In delay there lies no plenty; Then come kiss me, sweet and twenty, Youth's a stuff will not endure.
lines: [Sings]
What is love? 'tis not hereafter;
Present mirth hath present laughter;
What's to come is still unsure:
In delay there lies no plenty;
Then come kiss me, sweet and twenty,
Youth's a stuff will not endure.

speeches.speech: A mellifluous voice, as I am true knight.
lines: A mellifluous voice, as I am true knight.

speeches.speech: A contagious breath.
lines: A contagious breath.

speeches.speech: Very sweet and contagious, i' faith.
lines: Very sweet and contagious, i' faith.

speeches.speech: To hear by the nose, it is dulcet in contagion. But shall we make the welkin dance indeed? shall we rouse the night-owl in a catch that will draw three souls out of one weaver? shall we do that?
lines: To hear by the nose, it is dulcet in contagion.
But shall we make the welkin dance indeed? shall we
rouse the night-owl in a catch that will draw three
souls out of one weaver? shall we do that?

speeches.speech: An you love me, let's do't: I am dog at a catch.
lines: An you love me, let's do't: I am dog at a catch.

speeches.speech: By'r lady, sir, and some dogs will catch well.
lines: By'r lady, sir, and some dogs will catch well.

speeches.speech: Most certain. Let our catch be, 'Thou knave.'
lines: Most certain. Let our catch be, 'Thou knave.'

speeches.speech: 'Hold thy peace, thou knave,' knight? I shall be constrained in't to call thee knave, knight.
lines: 'Hold thy peace, thou knave,' knight? I shall be
constrained in't to call thee knave, knight.

speeches.speech: 'Tis not the first time I have constrained one to call me knave. Begin, fool: it begins 'Hold thy peace.'
lines: 'Tis not the first time I have constrained one to
call me knave. Begin, fool: it begins 'Hold thy peace.'

speeches.speech: I shall never begin if I hold my peace.
lines: I shall never begin if I hold my peace.

speeches.speech: Good, i' faith. Come, begin.
lines: Good, i' faith. Come, begin.

speeches.speech: What a caterwauling do you keep here! If my lady have not called up her steward Malvolio and bid him turn you out of doors, never trust me.
lines: What a caterwauling do you keep here! If my lady
have not called up her steward Malvolio and bid him
turn you out of doors, never trust me.

speeches.speech: My lady's a Cataian, we are politicians, Malvolio's a Peg-a-Ramsey, and 'Three merry men be we.' Am not I consanguineous? am I not of her blood? Tillyvally. Lady! [Sings] 'There dwelt a man in Babylon, lady, lady!'
lines: My lady's a Cataian, we are politicians, Malvolio's
a Peg-a-Ramsey, and 'Three merry men be we.' Am not
I consanguineous? am I not of her blood?
Tillyvally. Lady!
[Sings]
'There dwelt a man in Babylon, lady, lady!'

speeches.speech: Beshrew me, the knight's in admirable fooling.
lines: Beshrew me, the knight's in admirable fooling.

speeches.speech: Ay, he does well enough if he be disposed, and so do I too: he does it with a better grace, but I do it more natural.
lines: Ay, he does well enough if he be disposed, and so do
I too: he does it with a better grace, but I do it
more natural.

speeches.speech: [Sings] 'O, the twelfth day of December,'--
lines: [Sings] 'O, the twelfth day of December,'--

speeches.speech: For the love o' God, peace!
lines: For the love o' God, peace!

speeches.speech: My masters, are you mad? or what are you? Have ye no wit, manners, nor honesty, but to gabble like tinkers at this time of night? Do ye make an alehouse of my lady's house, that ye squeak out your coziers' catches without any mitigation or remorse of voice? Is there no respect of place, persons, nor time in you?
lines: My masters, are you mad? or what are you? Have ye
no wit, manners, nor honesty, but to gabble like
tinkers at this time of night? Do ye make an
alehouse of my lady's house, that ye squeak out your
coziers' catches without any mitigation or remorse
of voice? Is there no respect of place, persons, nor
time in you?

speeches.speech: We did keep time, sir, in our catches. Sneck up!
lines: We did keep time, sir, in our catches. Sneck up!

speeches.speech: Sir Toby, I must be round with you. My lady bade me tell you, that, though she harbours you as her kinsman, she's nothing allied to your disorders. If you can separate yourself and your misdemeanors, you are welcome to the house; if not, an it would please you to take leave of her, she is very willing to bid you farewell.
lines: Sir Toby, I must be round with you. My lady bade me
tell you, that, though she harbours you as her
kinsman, she's nothing allied to your disorders. If
you can separate yourself and your misdemeanors, you
are welcome to the house; if not, an it would please
you to take leave of her, she is very willing to bid
you farewell.

speeches.speech: 'Farewell, dear heart, since I must needs be gone.'
lines: 'Farewell, dear heart, since I must needs be gone.'

speeches.speech: Nay, good Sir Toby.
lines: Nay, good Sir Toby.

speeches.speech: 'His eyes do show his days are almost done.'
lines: 'His eyes do show his days are almost done.'

speeches.speech: Is't even so?
lines: Is't even so?

speeches.speech: 'But I will never die.'
lines: 'But I will never die.'

speeches.speech: Sir Toby, there you lie.
lines: Sir Toby, there you lie.

speeches.speech: This is much credit to you.
lines: This is much credit to you.

speeches.speech: 'Shall I bid him go?'
lines: 'Shall I bid him go?'

speeches.speech: 'What an if you do?'
lines: 'What an if you do?'

speeches.speech: 'Shall I bid him go, and spare not?'
lines: 'Shall I bid him go, and spare not?'

speeches.speech: 'O no, no, no, no, you dare not.'
lines: 'O no, no, no, no, you dare not.'

speeches.speech: Out o' tune, sir: ye lie. Art any more than a steward? Dost thou think, because thou art virtuous, there shall be no more cakes and ale?
lines: Out o' tune, sir: ye lie. Art any more than a
steward? Dost thou think, because thou art
virtuous, there shall be no more cakes and ale?

speeches.speech: Yes, by Saint Anne, and ginger shall be hot i' the mouth too.
lines: Yes, by Saint Anne, and ginger shall be hot i' the
mouth too.

speeches.speech: Thou'rt i' the right. Go, sir, rub your chain with crumbs. A stoup of wine, Maria!
lines: Thou'rt i' the right. Go, sir, rub your chain with
crumbs. A stoup of wine, Maria!

speeches.speech: Mistress Mary, if you prized my lady's favour at any thing more than contempt, you would not give means for this uncivil rule: she shall know of it, by this hand.
lines: Mistress Mary, if you prized my lady's favour at any
thing more than contempt, you would not give means
for this uncivil rule: she shall know of it, by this hand.

speeches.speech: Go shake your ears.
lines: Go shake your ears.

speeches.speech: 'Twere as good a deed as to drink when a man's a-hungry, to challenge him the field, and then to break promise with him and make a fool of him.
lines: 'Twere as good a deed as to drink when a man's
a-hungry, to challenge him the field, and then to
break promise with him and make a fool of him.

speeches.speech: Do't, knight: I'll write thee a challenge: or I'll deliver thy indignation to him by word of mouth.
lines: Do't, knight: I'll write thee a challenge: or I'll
deliver thy indignation to him by word of mouth.

speeches.speech: Sweet Sir Toby, be patient for tonight: since the youth of the count's was today with thy lady, she is much out of quiet. For Monsieur Malvolio, let me alone with him: if I do not gull him into a nayword, and make him a common recreation, do not think I have wit enough to lie straight in my bed: I know I can do it.
lines: Sweet Sir Toby, be patient for tonight: since the
youth of the count's was today with thy lady, she is
much out of quiet. For Monsieur Malvolio, let me
alone with him: if I do not gull him into a
nayword, and make him a common recreation, do not
think I have wit enough to lie straight in my bed:
I know I can do it.

speeches.speech: Possess us, possess us; tell us something of him.
lines: Possess us, possess us; tell us something of him.

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, sometimes he is a kind of puritan.
lines: Marry, sir, sometimes he is a kind of puritan.

speeches.speech: O, if I thought that I'ld beat him like a dog!
lines: O, if I thought that I'ld beat him like a dog!

speeches.speech: What, for being a puritan? thy exquisite reason, dear knight?
lines: What, for being a puritan? thy exquisite reason,
dear knight?

speeches.speech: I have no exquisite reason for't, but I have reason good enough.
lines: I have no exquisite reason for't, but I have reason
good enough.

speeches.speech: The devil a puritan that he is, or any thing constantly, but a time-pleaser; an affectioned ass, that cons state without book and utters it by great swarths: the best persuaded of himself, so crammed, as he thinks, with excellencies, that it is his grounds of faith that all that look on him love him; and on that vice in him will my revenge find notable cause to work.
lines: The devil a puritan that he is, or any thing
constantly, but a time-pleaser; an affectioned ass,
that cons state without book and utters it by great
swarths: the best persuaded of himself, so
crammed, as he thinks, with excellencies, that it is
his grounds of faith that all that look on him love
him; and on that vice in him will my revenge find
notable cause to work.

speeches.speech: What wilt thou do?
lines: What wilt thou do?

speeches.speech: I will drop in his way some obscure epistles of love; wherein, by the colour of his beard, the shape of his leg, the manner of his gait, the expressure of his eye, forehead, and complexion, he shall find himself most feelingly personated. I can write very like my lady your niece: on a forgotten matter we can hardly make distinction of our hands.
lines: I will drop in his way some obscure epistles of
love; wherein, by the colour of his beard, the shape
of his leg, the manner of his gait, the expressure
of his eye, forehead, and complexion, he shall find
himself most feelingly personated. I can write very
like my lady your niece: on a forgotten matter we
can hardly make distinction of our hands.

speeches.speech: Excellent! I smell a device.
lines: Excellent! I smell a device.

speeches.speech: I have't in my nose too.
lines: I have't in my nose too.

speeches.speech: He shall think, by the letters that thou wilt drop, that they come from my niece, and that she's in love with him.
lines: He shall think, by the letters that thou wilt drop,
that they come from my niece, and that she's in
love with him.

speeches.speech: My purpose is, indeed, a horse of that colour.
lines: My purpose is, indeed, a horse of that colour.

speeches.speech: And your horse now would make him an ass.
lines: And your horse now would make him an ass.

speeches.speech: Ass, I doubt not.
lines: Ass, I doubt not.

speeches.speech: O, 'twill be admirable!
lines: O, 'twill be admirable!

speeches.speech: Sport royal, I warrant you: I know my physic will work with him. I will plant you two, and let the fool make a third, where he shall find the letter: observe his construction of it. For this night, to bed, and dream on the event. Farewell.
lines: Sport royal, I warrant you: I know my physic will
work with him. I will plant you two, and let the
fool make a third, where he shall find the letter:
observe his construction of it. For this night, to
bed, and dream on the event. Farewell.

speeches.speech: Good night, Penthesilea.
lines: Good night, Penthesilea.

speeches.speech: Before me, she's a good wench.
lines: Before me, she's a good wench.

speeches.speech: She's a beagle, true-bred, and one that adores me: what o' that?
lines: She's a beagle, true-bred, and one that adores me:
what o' that?

speeches.speech: I was adored once too.
lines: I was adored once too.

speeches.speech: Let's to bed, knight. Thou hadst need send for more money.
lines: Let's to bed, knight. Thou hadst need send for
more money.

speeches.speech: If I cannot recover your niece, I am a foul way out.
lines: If I cannot recover your niece, I am a foul way out.

speeches.speech: Send for money, knight: if thou hast her not i' the end, call me cut.
lines: Send for money, knight: if thou hast her not i'
the end, call me cut.

speeches.speech: If I do not, never trust me, take it how you will.
lines: If I do not, never trust me, take it how you will.

speeches.speech: Come, come, I'll go burn some sack; 'tis too late to go to bed now: come, knight; come, knight.
lines: Come, come, I'll go burn some sack; 'tis too late
to go to bed now: come, knight; come, knight.

speeches.speech: Give me some music. Now, good morrow, friends. Now, good Cesario, but that piece of song, That old and antique song we heard last night: Methought it did relieve my passion much, More than light airs and recollected terms Of these most brisk and giddy-paced times: Come, but one verse.
lines: Give me some music. Now, good morrow, friends.
Now, good Cesario, but that piece of song,
That old and antique song we heard last night:
Methought it did relieve my passion much,
More than light airs and recollected terms
Of these most brisk and giddy-paced times:
Come, but one verse.

speeches.speech: He is not here, so please your lordship that should sing it.
lines: He is not here, so please your lordship that should sing it.

speeches.speech: Who was it?
lines: Who was it?

speeches.speech: Feste, the jester, my lord; a fool that the lady Olivia's father took much delight in. He is about the house.
lines: Feste, the jester, my lord; a fool that the lady
Olivia's father took much delight in. He is about the house.

speeches.speech: Seek him out, and play the tune the while. [Exit CURIO. Music plays] Come hither, boy: if ever thou shalt love, In the sweet pangs of it remember me; For such as I am all true lovers are, Unstaid and skittish in all motions else, Save in the constant image of the creature That is beloved. How dost thou like this tune?
lines: Seek him out, and play the tune the while.
[Exit CURIO. Music plays]
Come hither, boy: if ever thou shalt love,
In the sweet pangs of it remember me;
For such as I am all true lovers are,
Unstaid and skittish in all motions else,
Save in the constant image of the creature
That is beloved. How dost thou like this tune?

speeches.speech: It gives a very echo to the seat Where Love is throned.
lines: It gives a very echo to the seat
Where Love is throned.

speeches.speech: Thou dost speak masterly: My life upon't, young though thou art, thine eye Hath stay'd upon some favour that it loves: Hath it not, boy?
lines: Thou dost speak masterly:
My life upon't, young though thou art, thine eye
Hath stay'd upon some favour that it loves:
Hath it not, boy?

speeches.speech: A little, by your favour.
lines: A little, by your favour.

speeches.speech: What kind of woman is't?
lines: What kind of woman is't?

speeches.speech: Of your complexion.
lines: Of your complexion.
