speeches.speech: She is not worth thee, then. What years, i' faith?
lines: She is not worth thee, then. What years, i' faith?

speeches.speech: About your years, my lord.
lines: About your years, my lord.

speeches.speech: Too old by heaven: let still the woman take An elder than herself: so wears she to him, So sways she level in her husband's heart: For, boy, however we do praise ourselves, Our fancies are more giddy and unfirm, More longing, wavering, sooner lost and worn, Than women's are.
lines: Too old by heaven: let still the woman take
An elder than herself: so wears she to him,
So sways she level in her husband's heart:
For, boy, however we do praise ourselves,
Our fancies are more giddy and unfirm,
More longing, wavering, sooner lost and worn,
Than women's are.

speeches.speech: I think it well, my lord.
lines: I think it well, my lord.

speeches.speech: Then let thy love be younger than thyself, Or thy affection cannot hold the bent; For women are as roses, whose fair flower Being once display'd, doth fall that very hour.
lines: Then let thy love be younger than thyself,
Or thy affection cannot hold the bent;
For women are as roses, whose fair flower
Being once display'd, doth fall that very hour.

speeches.speech: And so they are: alas, that they are so; To die, even when they to perfection grow!
lines: And so they are: alas, that they are so;
To die, even when they to perfection grow!

speeches.speech: O, fellow, come, the song we had last night. Mark it, Cesario, it is old and plain; The spinsters and the knitters in the sun And the free maids that weave their thread with bones Do use to chant it: it is silly sooth, And dallies with the innocence of love, Like the old age.
lines: O, fellow, come, the song we had last night.
Mark it, Cesario, it is old and plain;
The spinsters and the knitters in the sun
And the free maids that weave their thread with bones
Do use to chant it: it is silly sooth,
And dallies with the innocence of love,
Like the old age.

speeches.speech: Are you ready, sir?
lines: Are you ready, sir?

speeches.speech: Ay; prithee, sing. [Music] SONG.
lines: Ay; prithee, sing.
[Music]
SONG.

speeches.speech: Come away, come away, death, And in sad cypress let me be laid; Fly away, fly away breath; I am slain by a fair cruel maid. My shroud of white, stuck all with yew, O, prepare it! My part of death, no one so true Did share it. Not a flower, not a flower sweet On my black coffin let there be strown; Not a friend, not a friend greet My poor corpse, where my bones shall be thrown: A thousand thousand sighs to save, Lay me, O, where Sad true lover never find my grave, To weep there!
lines: Come away, come away, death,
And in sad cypress let me be laid;
Fly away, fly away breath;
I am slain by a fair cruel maid.
My shroud of white, stuck all with yew,
O, prepare it!
My part of death, no one so true
Did share it.
Not a flower, not a flower sweet
On my black coffin let there be strown;
Not a friend, not a friend greet
My poor corpse, where my bones shall be thrown:
A thousand thousand sighs to save,
Lay me, O, where
Sad true lover never find my grave,
To weep there!

speeches.speech: There's for thy pains.
lines: There's for thy pains.

speeches.speech: No pains, sir: I take pleasure in singing, sir.
lines: No pains, sir: I take pleasure in singing, sir.

speeches.speech: I'll pay thy pleasure then.
lines: I'll pay thy pleasure then.

speeches.speech: Truly, sir, and pleasure will be paid, one time or another.
lines: Truly, sir, and pleasure will be paid, one time or another.

speeches.speech: Give me now leave to leave thee.
lines: Give me now leave to leave thee.

speeches.speech: Now, the melancholy god protect thee; and the tailor make thy doublet of changeable taffeta, for thy mind is a very opal. I would have men of such constancy put to sea, that their business might be every thing and their intent every where; for that's it that always makes a good voyage of nothing. Farewell.
lines: Now, the melancholy god protect thee; and the
tailor make thy doublet of changeable taffeta, for
thy mind is a very opal. I would have men of such
constancy put to sea, that their business might be
every thing and their intent every where; for that's
it that always makes a good voyage of nothing. Farewell.

speeches.speech: Let all the rest give place. [CURIO and Attendants retire] Once more, Cesario, Get thee to yond same sovereign cruelty: Tell her, my love, more noble than the world, Prizes not quantity of dirty lands; The parts that fortune hath bestow'd upon her, Tell her, I hold as giddily as fortune; But 'tis that miracle and queen of gems That nature pranks her in attracts my soul.
lines: Let all the rest give place.
[CURIO and Attendants retire]
Once more, Cesario,
Get thee to yond same sovereign cruelty:
Tell her, my love, more noble than the world,
Prizes not quantity of dirty lands;
The parts that fortune hath bestow'd upon her,
Tell her, I hold as giddily as fortune;
But 'tis that miracle and queen of gems
That nature pranks her in attracts my soul.

speeches.speech: But if she cannot love you, sir?
lines: But if she cannot love you, sir?

speeches.speech: I cannot be so answer'd.
lines: I cannot be so answer'd.

speeches.speech: Sooth, but you must. Say that some lady, as perhaps there is, Hath for your love a great a pang of heart As you have for Olivia: you cannot love her; You tell her so; must she not then be answer'd?
lines: Sooth, but you must.
Say that some lady, as perhaps there is,
Hath for your love a great a pang of heart
As you have for Olivia: you cannot love her;
You tell her so; must she not then be answer'd?

speeches.speech: There is no woman's sides Can bide the beating of so strong a passion As love doth give my heart; no woman's heart So big, to hold so much; they lack retention Alas, their love may be call'd appetite, No motion of the liver, but the palate, That suffer surfeit, cloyment and revolt; But mine is all as hungry as the sea, And can digest as much: make no compare Between that love a woman can bear me And that I owe Olivia.
lines: There is no woman's sides
Can bide the beating of so strong a passion
As love doth give my heart; no woman's heart
So big, to hold so much; they lack retention
Alas, their love may be call'd appetite,
No motion of the liver, but the palate,
That suffer surfeit, cloyment and revolt;
But mine is all as hungry as the sea,
And can digest as much: make no compare
Between that love a woman can bear me
And that I owe Olivia.

speeches.speech: Ay, but I know--
lines: Ay, but I know--

speeches.speech: What dost thou know?
lines: What dost thou know?

speeches.speech: Too well what love women to men may owe: In faith, they are as true of heart as we. My father had a daughter loved a man, As it might be, perhaps, were I a woman, I should your lordship.
lines: Too well what love women to men may owe:
In faith, they are as true of heart as we.
My father had a daughter loved a man,
As it might be, perhaps, were I a woman,
I should your lordship.

speeches.speech: And what's her history?
lines: And what's her history?

speeches.speech: A blank, my lord. She never told her love, But let concealment, like a worm i' the bud, Feed on her damask cheek: she pined in thought, And with a green and yellow melancholy She sat like patience on a monument, Smiling at grief. Was not this love indeed? We men may say more, swear more: but indeed Our shows are more than will; for still we prove Much in our vows, but little in our love.
lines: A blank, my lord. She never told her love,
But let concealment, like a worm i' the bud,
Feed on her damask cheek: she pined in thought,
And with a green and yellow melancholy
She sat like patience on a monument,
Smiling at grief. Was not this love indeed?
We men may say more, swear more: but indeed
Our shows are more than will; for still we prove
Much in our vows, but little in our love.

speeches.speech: But died thy sister of her love, my boy?
lines: But died thy sister of her love, my boy?

speeches.speech: I am all the daughters of my father's house, And all the brothers too: and yet I know not. Sir, shall I to this lady?
lines: I am all the daughters of my father's house,
And all the brothers too: and yet I know not.
Sir, shall I to this lady?

speeches.speech: Ay, that's the theme. To her in haste; give her this jewel; say, My love can give no place, bide no denay.
lines: Ay, that's the theme.
To her in haste; give her this jewel; say,
My love can give no place, bide no denay.

speeches.speech: Come thy ways, Signior Fabian.
lines: Come thy ways, Signior Fabian.

speeches.speech: Nay, I'll come: if I lose a scruple of this sport, let me be boiled to death with melancholy.
lines: Nay, I'll come: if I lose a scruple of this sport,
let me be boiled to death with melancholy.

speeches.speech: Wouldst thou not be glad to have the niggardly rascally sheep-biter come by some notable shame?
lines: Wouldst thou not be glad to have the niggardly
rascally sheep-biter come by some notable shame?

speeches.speech: I would exult, man: you know, he brought me out o' favour with my lady about a bear-baiting here.
lines: I would exult, man: you know, he brought me out o'
favour with my lady about a bear-baiting here.

speeches.speech: To anger him we'll have the bear again; and we will fool him black and blue: shall we not, Sir Andrew?
lines: To anger him we'll have the bear again; and we will
fool him black and blue: shall we not, Sir Andrew?

speeches.speech: An we do not, it is pity of our lives.
lines: An we do not, it is pity of our lives.

speeches.speech: Here comes the little villain. [Enter MARIA] How now, my metal of India!
lines: Here comes the little villain.
[Enter MARIA]
How now, my metal of India!

speeches.speech: Get ye all three into the box-tree: Malvolio's coming down this walk: he has been yonder i' the sun practising behavior to his own shadow this half hour: observe him, for the love of mockery; for I know this letter will make a contemplative idiot of him. Close, in the name of jesting! Lie thou there, [Throws down a letter] for here comes the trout that must be caught with tickling.
lines: Get ye all three into the box-tree: Malvolio's
coming down this walk: he has been yonder i' the
sun practising behavior to his own shadow this half
hour: observe him, for the love of mockery; for I
know this letter will make a contemplative idiot of

speeches.speech: 'Tis but fortune; all is fortune. Maria once told me she did affect me: and I have heard herself come thus near, that, should she fancy, it should be one of my complexion. Besides, she uses me with a more exalted respect than any one else that follows her. What should I think on't?
lines: 'Tis but fortune; all is fortune. Maria once told
me she did affect me: and I have heard herself come
thus near, that, should she fancy, it should be one
of my complexion. Besides, she uses me with a more
exalted respect than any one else that follows her.
What should I think on't?

speeches.speech: Here's an overweening rogue!
lines: Here's an overweening rogue!

speeches.speech: O, peace! Contemplation makes a rare turkey-cock of him: how he jets under his advanced plumes!
lines: O, peace! Contemplation makes a rare turkey-cock
of him: how he jets under his advanced plumes!

speeches.speech: 'Slight, I could so beat the rogue!
lines: 'Slight, I could so beat the rogue!

speeches.speech: Peace, I say.
lines: Peace, I say.

speeches.speech: To be Count Malvolio!
lines: To be Count Malvolio!

speeches.speech: Ah, rogue!
lines: Ah, rogue!

speeches.speech: Pistol him, pistol him.
lines: Pistol him, pistol him.

speeches.speech: Peace, peace!
lines: Peace, peace!

speeches.speech: There is example for't; the lady of the Strachy married the yeoman of the wardrobe.
lines: There is example for't; the lady of the Strachy
married the yeoman of the wardrobe.

speeches.speech: Fie on him, Jezebel!
lines: Fie on him, Jezebel!

speeches.speech: O, peace! now he's deeply in: look how imagination blows him.
lines: O, peace! now he's deeply in: look how
imagination blows him.

speeches.speech: Having been three months married to her, sitting in my state,--
lines: Having been three months married to her, sitting in
my state,--

speeches.speech: O, for a stone-bow, to hit him in the eye!
lines: O, for a stone-bow, to hit him in the eye!

speeches.speech: Calling my officers about me, in my branched velvet gown; having come from a day-bed, where I have left Olivia sleeping,--
lines: Calling my officers about me, in my branched velvet
gown; having come from a day-bed, where I have left
Olivia sleeping,--

speeches.speech: Fire and brimstone!
lines: Fire and brimstone!

speeches.speech: O, peace, peace!
lines: O, peace, peace!

speeches.speech: And then to have the humour of state; and after a demure travel of regard, telling them I know my place as I would they should do theirs, to for my kinsman Toby,--
lines: And then to have the humour of state; and after a
demure travel of regard, telling them I know my
place as I would they should do theirs, to for my
kinsman Toby,--

speeches.speech: Bolts and shackles!
lines: Bolts and shackles!

speeches.speech: O peace, peace, peace! now, now.
lines: O peace, peace, peace! now, now.

speeches.speech: Seven of my people, with an obedient start, make out for him: I frown the while; and perchance wind up watch, or play with my--some rich jewel. Toby approaches; courtesies there to me,--
lines: Seven of my people, with an obedient start, make
out for him: I frown the while; and perchance wind
up watch, or play with my--some rich jewel. Toby
approaches; courtesies there to me,--

speeches.speech: Shall this fellow live?
lines: Shall this fellow live?

speeches.speech: Though our silence be drawn from us with cars, yet peace.
lines: Though our silence be drawn from us with cars, yet peace.

speeches.speech: I extend my hand to him thus, quenching my familiar smile with an austere regard of control,--
lines: I extend my hand to him thus, quenching my familiar
smile with an austere regard of control,--

speeches.speech: And does not Toby take you a blow o' the lips then?
lines: And does not Toby take you a blow o' the lips then?

speeches.speech: Saying, 'Cousin Toby, my fortunes having cast me on your niece give me this prerogative of speech,'--
lines: Saying, 'Cousin Toby, my fortunes having cast me on
your niece give me this prerogative of speech,'--

speeches.speech: What, what?
lines: What, what?

speeches.speech: 'You must amend your drunkenness.'
lines: 'You must amend your drunkenness.'

speeches.speech: Out, scab!
lines: Out, scab!

speeches.speech: Nay, patience, or we break the sinews of our plot.
lines: Nay, patience, or we break the sinews of our plot.

speeches.speech: 'Besides, you waste the treasure of your time with a foolish knight,'--
lines: 'Besides, you waste the treasure of your time with
a foolish knight,'--

speeches.speech: That's me, I warrant you.
lines: That's me, I warrant you.

speeches.speech: 'One Sir Andrew,'--
lines: 'One Sir Andrew,'--

speeches.speech: I knew 'twas I; for many do call me fool.
lines: I knew 'twas I; for many do call me fool.

speeches.speech: What employment have we here?
lines: What employment have we here?

speeches.speech: Now is the woodcock near the gin.
lines: Now is the woodcock near the gin.

speeches.speech: O, peace! and the spirit of humour intimate reading aloud to him!
lines: O, peace! and the spirit of humour intimate reading
aloud to him!

speeches.speech: By my life, this is my lady's hand these be her very C's, her U's and her T's and thus makes she her great P's. It is, in contempt of question, her hand.
lines: By my life, this is my lady's hand these be her
very C's, her U's and her T's and thus makes she her
great P's. It is, in contempt of question, her hand.

speeches.speech: Her C's, her U's and her T's: why that?
lines: Her C's, her U's and her T's: why that?

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'To the unknown beloved, this, and my good wishes:'--her very phrases! By your leave, wax. Soft! and the impressure her Lucrece, with which she uses to seal: 'tis my lady. To whom should this be?
lines: [Reads] 'To the unknown beloved, this, and my good
wishes:'--her very phrases! By your leave, wax.
Soft! and the impressure her Lucrece, with which she
uses to seal: 'tis my lady. To whom should this be?

speeches.speech: This wins him, liver and all.
lines: This wins him, liver and all.

speeches.speech: [Reads] Jove knows I love: But who? Lips, do not move; No man must know. 'No man must know.' What follows? the numbers altered! 'No man must know:' if this should be thee, Malvolio?
lines: [Reads]
Jove knows I love: But who?
Lips, do not move;
No man must know.
'No man must know.' What follows? the numbers
altered! 'No man must know:' if this should be
thee, Malvolio?

speeches.speech: Marry, hang thee, brock!
lines: Marry, hang thee, brock!

speeches.speech: [Reads] I may command where I adore; But silence, like a Lucrece knife, With bloodless stroke my heart doth gore: M, O, A, I, doth sway my life.
lines: [Reads]
I may command where I adore;
But silence, like a Lucrece knife,
With bloodless stroke my heart doth gore:
M, O, A, I, doth sway my life.

speeches.speech: A fustian riddle!
lines: A fustian riddle!

speeches.speech: Excellent wench, say I.
lines: Excellent wench, say I.

speeches.speech: 'M, O, A, I, doth sway my life.' Nay, but first, let me see, let me see, let me see.
lines: 'M, O, A, I, doth sway my life.' Nay, but first, let
me see, let me see, let me see.

speeches.speech: What dish o' poison has she dressed him!
lines: What dish o' poison has she dressed him!

speeches.speech: And with what wing the staniel cheques at it!
lines: And with what wing the staniel cheques at it!

speeches.speech: 'I may command where I adore.' Why, she may command me: I serve her; she is my lady. Why, this is evident to any formal capacity; there is no obstruction in this: and the end,--what should that alphabetical position portend? If I could make that resemble something in me,--Softly! M, O, A, I,--
lines: 'I may command where I adore.' Why, she may command
me: I serve her; she is my lady. Why, this is
evident to any formal capacity; there is no

that alphabetical position portend? If I could make

speeches.speech: O, ay, make up that: he is now at a cold scent.
lines: O, ay, make up that: he is now at a cold scent.

speeches.speech: Sowter will cry upon't for all this, though it be as rank as a fox.
lines: Sowter will cry upon't for all this, though it be as
rank as a fox.

speeches.speech: M,--Malvolio; M,--why, that begins my name.
lines: M,--Malvolio; M,--why, that begins my name.

speeches.speech: Did not I say he would work it out? the cur is excellent at faults.
lines: Did not I say he would work it out? the cur is
excellent at faults.

speeches.speech: M,--but then there is no consonancy in the sequel; that suffers under probation A should follow but O does.
lines: M,--but then there is no consonancy in the sequel;
that suffers under probation A should follow but O does.

speeches.speech: And O shall end, I hope.
lines: And O shall end, I hope.

speeches.speech: Ay, or I'll cudgel him, and make him cry O!
lines: Ay, or I'll cudgel him, and make him cry O!

speeches.speech: And then I comes behind.
lines: And then I comes behind.

speeches.speech: Ay, an you had any eye behind you, you might see more detraction at your heels than fortunes before you.
lines: Ay, an you had any eye behind you, you might see
more detraction at your heels than fortunes before
you.

speeches.speech: M, O, A, I; this simulation is not as the former: and yet, to crush this a little, it would bow to me, for every one of these letters are in my name. Soft! here follows prose. [Reads] 'If this fall into thy hand, revolve. In my stars I am above thee; but be not afraid of greatness: some are born great, some achieve greatness, and some have greatness thrust upon 'em. Thy Fates open their hands; let thy blood and spirit embrace them; and, to inure thyself to what thou art like to be, cast thy humble slough and appear fresh. Be opposite with a kinsman, surly with servants; let thy tongue tang arguments of state; put thyself into the trick of singularity: she thus advises thee that sighs for thee. Remember who commended thy yellow stockings, and wished to see thee ever cross-gartered: I say, remember. Go to, thou art made, if thou desirest to be so; if not, let me see thee a steward still, the fellow of servants, and not worthy to touch Fortune's fingers. Farewell. She that would alter services with thee, THE FORTUNATE-UNHAPPY.' Daylight and champaign discovers not more: this is open. I will be proud, I will read politic authors, I will baffle Sir Toby, I will wash off gross acquaintance, I will be point-devise the very man. I do not now fool myself, to let imagination jade me; for every reason excites to this, that my lady loves me. She did commend my yellow stockings of late, she did praise my leg being cross-gartered; and in this she manifests herself to my love, and with a kind of injunction drives me to these habits of her liking. I thank my stars I am happy. I will be strange, stout, in yellow stockings, and cross-gartered, even with the swiftness of putting on. Jove and my stars be praised! Here is yet a postscript. [Reads] 'Thou canst not choose but know who I am. If thou entertainest my love, let it appear in thy smiling; thy smiles become thee well; therefore in my presence still smile, dear my sweet, I prithee.' Jove, I thank thee: I will smile; I will do everything that thou wilt have me.

yet, to crush this a little, it would bow to me, for
every one of these letters are in my name. Soft!
here follows prose.
[Reads]

THE FORTUNATE-UNHAPPY.'

speeches.speech: I will not give my part of this sport for a pension of thousands to be paid from the Sophy.
lines: I will not give my part of this sport for a pension
of thousands to be paid from the Sophy.

speeches.speech: I could marry this wench for this device.
lines: I could marry this wench for this device.

speeches.speech: So could I too.
lines: So could I too.

speeches.speech: And ask no other dowry with her but such another jest.
lines: And ask no other dowry with her but such another jest.

speeches.speech: Nor I neither.
lines: Nor I neither.

speeches.speech: Here comes my noble gull-catcher.
lines: Here comes my noble gull-catcher.

speeches.speech: Wilt thou set thy foot o' my neck?
lines: Wilt thou set thy foot o' my neck?

speeches.speech: Or o' mine either?
lines: Or o' mine either?

speeches.speech: Shall I play my freedom at traytrip, and become thy bond-slave?
lines: Shall I play my freedom at traytrip, and become thy
bond-slave?

speeches.speech: I' faith, or I either?
lines: I' faith, or I either?

speeches.speech: Why, thou hast put him in such a dream, that when the image of it leaves him he must run mad.
lines: Why, thou hast put him in such a dream, that when
the image of it leaves him he must run mad.

speeches.speech: Nay, but say true; does it work upon him?
lines: Nay, but say true; does it work upon him?

speeches.speech: Like aqua-vitae with a midwife.
lines: Like aqua-vitae with a midwife.

speeches.speech: If you will then see the fruits of the sport, mark his first approach before my lady: he will come to her in yellow stockings, and 'tis a colour she abhors, and cross-gartered, a fashion she detests; and he will smile upon her, which will now be so unsuitable to her disposition, being addicted to a melancholy as she is, that it cannot but turn him into a notable contempt. If you will see it, follow me.
lines: If you will then see the fruits of the sport, mark
his first approach before my lady: he will come to
her in yellow stockings, and 'tis a colour she
abhors, and cross-gartered, a fashion she detests;
and he will smile upon her, which will now be so
unsuitable to her disposition, being addicted to a
melancholy as she is, that it cannot but turn him
into a notable contempt. If you will see it, follow
me.

speeches.speech: To the gates of Tartar, thou most excellent devil of wit!
lines: To the gates of Tartar, thou most excellent devil of wit!

speeches.speech: I'll make one too.
lines: I'll make one too.

speeches.speech: Save thee, friend, and thy music: dost thou live by thy tabour?
lines: Save thee, friend, and thy music: dost thou live by
thy tabour?

speeches.speech: No, sir, I live by the church.
lines: No, sir, I live by the church.

speeches.speech: Art thou a churchman?
lines: Art thou a churchman?

speeches.speech: No such matter, sir: I do live by the church; for I do live at my house, and my house doth stand by the church.
lines: No such matter, sir: I do live by the church; for
I do live at my house, and my house doth stand by
the church.

speeches.speech: So thou mayst say, the king lies by a beggar, if a beggar dwell near him; or, the church stands by thy tabour, if thy tabour stand by the church.
lines: So thou mayst say, the king lies by a beggar, if a
beggar dwell near him; or, the church stands by thy
tabour, if thy tabour stand by the church.

speeches.speech: You have said, sir. To see this age! A sentence is but a cheveril glove to a good wit: how quickly the wrong side may be turned outward!
lines: You have said, sir. To see this age! A sentence is
but a cheveril glove to a good wit: how quickly the
wrong side may be turned outward!

speeches.speech: Nay, that's certain; they that dally nicely with words may quickly make them wanton.
lines: Nay, that's certain; they that dally nicely with
words may quickly make them wanton.

speeches.speech: I would, therefore, my sister had had no name, sir.
lines: I would, therefore, my sister had had no name, sir.

speeches.speech: Why, man?
lines: Why, man?

speeches.speech: Why, sir, her name's a word; and to dally with that word might make my sister wanton. But indeed words are very rascals since bonds disgraced them.
lines: Why, sir, her name's a word; and to dally with that
word might make my sister wanton. But indeed words
are very rascals since bonds disgraced them.

speeches.speech: Thy reason, man?
lines: Thy reason, man?

speeches.speech: Troth, sir, I can yield you none without words; and words are grown so false, I am loath to prove reason with them.
lines: Troth, sir, I can yield you none without words; and
words are grown so false, I am loath to prove
reason with them.

speeches.speech: I warrant thou art a merry fellow and carest for nothing.
lines: I warrant thou art a merry fellow and carest for nothing.

speeches.speech: Not so, sir, I do care for something; but in my conscience, sir, I do not care for you: if that be to care for nothing, sir, I would it would make you invisible.
lines: Not so, sir, I do care for something; but in my
conscience, sir, I do not care for you: if that be
to care for nothing, sir, I would it would make you invisible.

speeches.speech: Art not thou the Lady Olivia's fool?
lines: Art not thou the Lady Olivia's fool?

speeches.speech: No, indeed, sir; the Lady Olivia has no folly: she will keep no fool, sir, till she be married; and fools are as like husbands as pilchards are to herrings; the husband's the bigger: I am indeed not her fool, but her corrupter of words.
lines: No, indeed, sir; the Lady Olivia has no folly: she
will keep no fool, sir, till she be married; and
fools are as like husbands as pilchards are to
herrings; the husband's the bigger: I am indeed not
her fool, but her corrupter of words.

speeches.speech: I saw thee late at the Count Orsino's.
lines: I saw thee late at the Count Orsino's.

speeches.speech: Foolery, sir, does walk about the orb like the sun, it shines every where. I would be sorry, sir, but the fool should be as oft with your master as with my mistress: I think I saw your wisdom there.
lines: Foolery, sir, does walk about the orb like the sun,
it shines every where. I would be sorry, sir, but
the fool should be as oft with your master as with
my mistress: I think I saw your wisdom there.

speeches.speech: Nay, an thou pass upon me, I'll no more with thee. Hold, there's expenses for thee.
lines: Nay, an thou pass upon me, I'll no more with thee.
Hold, there's expenses for thee.

speeches.speech: Now Jove, in his next commodity of hair, send thee a beard!
lines: Now Jove, in his next commodity of hair, send thee a beard!

speeches.speech: By my troth, I'll tell thee, I am almost sick for one; [Aside] though I would not have it grow on my chin. Is thy lady within?
lines: By my troth, I'll tell thee, I am almost sick for
one;
[Aside]
though I would not have it grow on my chin. Is thy
lady within?

speeches.speech: Would not a pair of these have bred, sir?
lines: Would not a pair of these have bred, sir?

speeches.speech: Yes, being kept together and put to use.
lines: Yes, being kept together and put to use.

speeches.speech: I would play Lord Pandarus of Phrygia, sir, to bring a Cressida to this Troilus.
lines: I would play Lord Pandarus of Phrygia, sir, to bring
a Cressida to this Troilus.

speeches.speech: I understand you, sir; 'tis well begged.
lines: I understand you, sir; 'tis well begged.

speeches.speech: The matter, I hope, is not great, sir, begging but a beggar: Cressida was a beggar. My lady is within, sir. I will construe to them whence you come; who you are and what you would are out of my welkin, I might say 'element,' but the word is over-worn.
lines: The matter, I hope, is not great, sir, begging but
a beggar: Cressida was a beggar. My lady is
within, sir. I will construe to them whence you
come; who you are and what you would are out of my
welkin, I might say 'element,' but the word is over-worn.

speeches.speech: This fellow is wise enough to play the fool; And to do that well craves a kind of wit: He must observe their mood on whom he jests, The quality of persons, and the time, And, like the haggard, cheque at every feather That comes before his eye. This is a practise As full of labour as a wise man's art For folly that he wisely shows is fit; But wise men, folly-fall'n, quite taint their wit.
lines: This fellow is wise enough to play the fool;
And to do that well craves a kind of wit:
He must observe their mood on whom he jests,
The quality of persons, and the time,
And, like the haggard, cheque at every feather
That comes before his eye. This is a practise
As full of labour as a wise man's art
For folly that he wisely shows is fit;
But wise men, folly-fall'n, quite taint their wit.

speeches.speech: Save you, gentleman.
lines: Save you, gentleman.

speeches.speech: And you, sir.
lines: And you, sir.

speeches.speech: Dieu vous garde, monsieur.
lines: Dieu vous garde, monsieur.

speeches.speech: Et vous aussi; votre serviteur.
lines: Et vous aussi; votre serviteur.

speeches.speech: I hope, sir, you are; and I am yours.
lines: I hope, sir, you are; and I am yours.

speeches.speech: Will you encounter the house? my niece is desirous you should enter, if your trade be to her.
lines: Will you encounter the house? my niece is desirous
you should enter, if your trade be to her.

speeches.speech: I am bound to your niece, sir; I mean, she is the list of my voyage.
lines: I am bound to your niece, sir; I mean, she is the
list of my voyage.

speeches.speech: Taste your legs, sir; put them to motion.
lines: Taste your legs, sir; put them to motion.

speeches.speech: My legs do better understand me, sir, than I understand what you mean by bidding me taste my legs.
lines: My legs do better understand me, sir, than I
understand what you mean by bidding me taste my legs.

speeches.speech: I mean, to go, sir, to enter.
lines: I mean, to go, sir, to enter.

speeches.speech: I will answer you with gait and entrance. But we are prevented. [Enter OLIVIA and MARIA] Most excellent accomplished lady, the heavens rain odours on you!
lines: I will answer you with gait and entrance. But we
are prevented.
[Enter OLIVIA and MARIA]
Most excellent accomplished lady, the heavens rain
odours on you!

speeches.speech: That youth's a rare courtier: 'Rain odours;' well.
lines: That youth's a rare courtier: 'Rain odours;' well.

speeches.speech: My matter hath no voice, to your own most pregnant and vouchsafed ear.
lines: My matter hath no voice, to your own most pregnant
and vouchsafed ear.

speeches.speech: 'Odours,' 'pregnant' and 'vouchsafed:' I'll get 'em all three all ready.
lines: 'Odours,' 'pregnant' and 'vouchsafed:' I'll get 'em
all three all ready.

speeches.speech: Let the garden door be shut, and leave me to my hearing. [Exeunt SIR TOBY BELCH, SIR ANDREW, and MARIA] Give me your hand, sir.
lines: Let the garden door be shut, and leave me to my hearing.
[Exeunt SIR TOBY BELCH, SIR ANDREW, and MARIA]
Give me your hand, sir.

speeches.speech: My duty, madam, and most humble service.
lines: My duty, madam, and most humble service.

speeches.speech: What is your name?
lines: What is your name?

speeches.speech: Cesario is your servant's name, fair princess.
lines: Cesario is your servant's name, fair princess.

speeches.speech: My servant, sir! 'Twas never merry world Since lowly feigning was call'd compliment: You're servant to the Count Orsino, youth.
lines: My servant, sir! 'Twas never merry world
Since lowly feigning was call'd compliment:
You're servant to the Count Orsino, youth.

speeches.speech: And he is yours, and his must needs be yours: Your servant's servant is your servant, madam.
lines: And he is yours, and his must needs be yours:
Your servant's servant is your servant, madam.

speeches.speech: For him, I think not on him: for his thoughts, Would they were blanks, rather than fill'd with me!
lines: For him, I think not on him: for his thoughts,
Would they were blanks, rather than fill'd with me!

speeches.speech: Madam, I come to whet your gentle thoughts On his behalf.
lines: Madam, I come to whet your gentle thoughts
On his behalf.

speeches.speech: O, by your leave, I pray you, I bade you never speak again of him: But, would you undertake another suit, I had rather hear you to solicit that Than music from the spheres.
lines: O, by your leave, I pray you,
I bade you never speak again of him:
But, would you undertake another suit,
I had rather hear you to solicit that
Than music from the spheres.

speeches.speech: Dear lady,--
lines: Dear lady,--

speeches.speech: Give me leave, beseech you. I did send, After the last enchantment you did here, A ring in chase of you: so did I abuse Myself, my servant and, I fear me, you: Under your hard construction must I sit,To force that on you, in a shameful cunning, Which you knew none of yours: what might you think? Have you not set mine honour at the stake And baited it with all the unmuzzled thoughts That tyrannous heart can think? To one of your receiving Enough is shown: a cypress, not a bosom, Hideth my heart. So, let me hear you speak.
lines: Give me leave, beseech you. I did send,
After the last enchantment you did here,
A ring in chase of you: so did I abuse
Myself, my servant and, I fear me, you:
Under your hard construction must I sit,To force that on you, in a shameful cunning,
Which you knew none of yours: what might you think?
Have you not set mine honour at the stake
And baited it with all the unmuzzled thoughts
That tyrannous heart can think? To one of your receiving
Enough is shown: a cypress, not a bosom,
Hideth my heart. So, let me hear you speak.

speeches.speech: I pity you.
lines: I pity you.

speeches.speech: That's a degree to love.
lines: That's a degree to love.

speeches.speech: No, not a grize; for 'tis a vulgar proof, That very oft we pity enemies.
lines: No, not a grize; for 'tis a vulgar proof,
That very oft we pity enemies.

speeches.speech: Why, then, methinks 'tis time to smile again. O, world, how apt the poor are to be proud! If one should be a prey, how much the better To fall before the lion than the wolf! [Clock strikes] The clock upbraids me with the waste of time. Be not afraid, good youth, I will not have you: And yet, when wit and youth is come to harvest, Your were is alike to reap a proper man: There lies your way, due west.
lines: Why, then, methinks 'tis time to smile again.
O, world, how apt the poor are to be proud!
If one should be a prey, how much the better
To fall before the lion than the wolf!
[Clock strikes]
The clock upbraids me with the waste of time.
Be not afraid, good youth, I will not have you:
And yet, when wit and youth is come to harvest,
Your were is alike to reap a proper man:
There lies your way, due west.

speeches.speech: Then westward-ho! Grace and good disposition Attend your ladyship! You'll nothing, madam, to my lord by me?
lines: Then westward-ho! Grace and good disposition
Attend your ladyship!
You'll nothing, madam, to my lord by me?

speeches.speech: Stay: I prithee, tell me what thou thinkest of me.
lines: Stay:
I prithee, tell me what thou thinkest of me.

speeches.speech: That you do think you are not what you are.
lines: That you do think you are not what you are.

speeches.speech: If I think so, I think the same of you.
lines: If I think so, I think the same of you.

speeches.speech: Then think you right: I am not what I am.
lines: Then think you right: I am not what I am.

speeches.speech: I would you were as I would have you be!
lines: I would you were as I would have you be!

speeches.speech: Would it be better, madam, than I am? I wish it might, for now I am your fool.
lines: Would it be better, madam, than I am?
I wish it might, for now I am your fool.

speeches.speech: O, what a deal of scorn looks beautiful In the contempt and anger of his lip! A murderous guilt shows not itself more soon Than love that would seem hid: love's night is noon. Cesario, by the roses of the spring, By maidhood, honour, truth and every thing, I love thee so, that, maugre all thy pride, Nor wit nor reason can my passion hide. Do not extort thy reasons from this clause, For that I woo, thou therefore hast no cause, But rather reason thus with reason fetter, Love sought is good, but given unsought better.
lines: O, what a deal of scorn looks beautiful
In the contempt and anger of his lip!
A murderous guilt shows not itself more soon
Than love that would seem hid: love's night is noon.
Cesario, by the roses of the spring,
By maidhood, honour, truth and every thing,
I love thee so, that, maugre all thy pride,
Nor wit nor reason can my passion hide.
Do not extort thy reasons from this clause,
For that I woo, thou therefore hast no cause,
But rather reason thus with reason fetter,
Love sought is good, but given unsought better.

speeches.speech: By innocence I swear, and by my youth I have one heart, one bosom and one truth, And that no woman has; nor never none Shall mistress be of it, save I alone. And so adieu, good madam: never more Will I my master's tears to you deplore.
lines: By innocence I swear, and by my youth
I have one heart, one bosom and one truth,
And that no woman has; nor never none
Shall mistress be of it, save I alone.
And so adieu, good madam: never more
Will I my master's tears to you deplore.

speeches.speech: Yet come again; for thou perhaps mayst move That heart, which now abhors, to like his love.
lines: Yet come again; for thou perhaps mayst move
That heart, which now abhors, to like his love.

speeches.speech: No, faith, I'll not stay a jot longer.
lines: No, faith, I'll not stay a jot longer.

speeches.speech: Thy reason, dear venom, give thy reason.
lines: Thy reason, dear venom, give thy reason.

speeches.speech: You must needs yield your reason, Sir Andrew.
lines: You must needs yield your reason, Sir Andrew.

speeches.speech: Marry, I saw your niece do more favours to the count's serving-man than ever she bestowed upon me; I saw't i' the orchard.
lines: Marry, I saw your niece do more favours to the
count's serving-man than ever she bestowed upon me;
I saw't i' the orchard.

speeches.speech: Did she see thee the while, old boy? tell me that.
lines: Did she see thee the while, old boy? tell me that.

speeches.speech: As plain as I see you now.
lines: As plain as I see you now.

speeches.speech: This was a great argument of love in her toward you.
lines: This was a great argument of love in her toward you.

speeches.speech: 'Slight, will you make an ass o' me?
lines: 'Slight, will you make an ass o' me?

speeches.speech: I will prove it legitimate, sir, upon the oaths of judgment and reason.
lines: I will prove it legitimate, sir, upon the oaths of
judgment and reason.

speeches.speech: And they have been grand-jury-men since before Noah was a sailor.
lines: And they have been grand-jury-men since before Noah
was a sailor.

speeches.speech: She did show favour to the youth in your sight only to exasperate you, to awake your dormouse valour, to put fire in your heart and brimstone in your liver. You should then have accosted her; and with some excellent jests, fire-new from the mint, you should have banged the youth into dumbness. This was looked for at your hand, and this was balked: the double gilt of this opportunity you let time wash off, and you are now sailed into the north of my lady's opinion; where you will hang like an icicle on a Dutchman's beard, unless you do redeem it by some laudable attempt either of valour or policy.
lines: She did show favour to the youth in your sight only
to exasperate you, to awake your dormouse valour, to
put fire in your heart and brimstone in your liver.
You should then have accosted her; and with some
excellent jests, fire-new from the mint, you should
have banged the youth into dumbness. This was
looked for at your hand, and this was balked: the
double gilt of this opportunity you let time wash
off, and you are now sailed into the north of my
lady's opinion; where you will hang like an icicle
on a Dutchman's beard, unless you do redeem it by
some laudable attempt either of valour or policy.

speeches.speech: An't be any way, it must be with valour; for policy I hate: I had as lief be a Brownist as a politician.
lines: An't be any way, it must be with valour; for policy
I hate: I had as lief be a Brownist as a
politician.

speeches.speech: Why, then, build me thy fortunes upon the basis of valour. Challenge me the count's youth to fight with him; hurt him in eleven places: my niece shall take note of it; and assure thyself, there is no love-broker in the world can more prevail in man's commendation with woman than report of valour.
lines: Why, then, build me thy fortunes upon the basis of
valour. Challenge me the count's youth to fight
with him; hurt him in eleven places: my niece shall
take note of it; and assure thyself, there is no
love-broker in the world can more prevail in man's
commendation with woman than report of valour.

speeches.speech: There is no way but this, Sir Andrew.
lines: There is no way but this, Sir Andrew.

speeches.speech: Will either of you bear me a challenge to him?
lines: Will either of you bear me a challenge to him?

speeches.speech: Go, write it in a martial hand; be curst and brief; it is no matter how witty, so it be eloquent and fun of invention: taunt him with the licence of ink: if thou thou'st him some thrice, it shall not be amiss; and as many lies as will lie in thy sheet of paper, although the sheet were big enough for the bed of Ware in England, set 'em down: go, about it. Let there be gall enough in thy ink, though thou write with a goose-pen, no matter: about it.
lines: Go, write it in a martial hand; be curst and brief;
it is no matter how witty, so it be eloquent and fun
of invention: taunt him with the licence of ink:
if thou thou'st him some thrice, it shall not be
amiss; and as many lies as will lie in thy sheet of
paper, although the sheet were big enough for the
bed of Ware in England, set 'em down: go, about it.
Let there be gall enough in thy ink, though thou
write with a goose-pen, no matter: about it.

speeches.speech: Where shall I find you?
lines: Where shall I find you?

speeches.speech: We'll call thee at the cubiculo: go.
lines: We'll call thee at the cubiculo: go.

speeches.speech: This is a dear manikin to you, Sir Toby.
lines: This is a dear manikin to you, Sir Toby.

speeches.speech: I have been dear to him, lad, some two thousand strong, or so.
lines: I have been dear to him, lad, some two thousand
strong, or so.

speeches.speech: We shall have a rare letter from him: but you'll not deliver't?
lines: We shall have a rare letter from him: but you'll
not deliver't?

speeches.speech: Never trust me, then; and by all means stir on the youth to an answer. I think oxen and wainropes cannot hale them together. For Andrew, if he were opened, and you find so much blood in his liver as will clog the foot of a flea, I'll eat the rest of the anatomy.
lines: Never trust me, then; and by all means stir on the
youth to an answer. I think oxen and wainropes
cannot hale them together. For Andrew, if he were
opened, and you find so much blood in his liver as
will clog the foot of a flea, I'll eat the rest of
the anatomy.

speeches.speech: And his opposite, the youth, bears in his visage no great presage of cruelty.
lines: And his opposite, the youth, bears in his visage no
great presage of cruelty.

speeches.speech: Look, where the youngest wren of nine comes.
lines: Look, where the youngest wren of nine comes.

speeches.speech: If you desire the spleen, and will laugh yourself into stitches, follow me. Yond gull Malvolio is turned heathen, a very renegado; for there is no Christian, that means to be saved by believing rightly, can ever believe such impossible passages of grossness. He's in yellow stockings.
lines: If you desire the spleen, and will laugh yourself
into stitches, follow me. Yond gull Malvolio is
turned heathen, a very renegado; for there is no
Christian, that means to be saved by believing
rightly, can ever believe such impossible passages
of grossness. He's in yellow stockings.

speeches.speech: And cross-gartered?
lines: And cross-gartered?

speeches.speech: Most villanously; like a pedant that keeps a school i' the church. I have dogged him, like his murderer. He does obey every point of the letter that I dropped to betray him: he does smile his face into more lines than is in the new map with the augmentation of the Indies: you have not seen such a thing as 'tis. I can hardly forbear hurling things at him. I know my lady will strike him: if she do, he'll smile and take't for a great favour.
lines: Most villanously; like a pedant that keeps a school
i' the church. I have dogged him, like his
murderer. He does obey every point of the letter
that I dropped to betray him: he does smile his
face into more lines than is in the new map with the
augmentation of the Indies: you have not seen such
a thing as 'tis. I can hardly forbear hurling things
at him. I know my lady will strike him: if she do,
he'll smile and take't for a great favour.

speeches.speech: Come, bring us, bring us where he is.
lines: Come, bring us, bring us where he is.

speeches.speech: I would not by my will have troubled you; But, since you make your pleasure of your pains, I will no further chide you.
lines: I would not by my will have troubled you;
But, since you make your pleasure of your pains,
I will no further chide you.

speeches.speech: I could not stay behind you: my desire, More sharp than filed steel, did spur me forth; And not all love to see you, though so much As might have drawn one to a longer voyage, But jealousy what might befall your travel, Being skilless in these parts; which to a stranger, Unguided and unfriended, often prove Rough and unhospitable: my willing love, The rather by these arguments of fear, Set forth in your pursuit.
lines: I could not stay behind you: my desire,
More sharp than filed steel, did spur me forth;
And not all love to see you, though so much
As might have drawn one to a longer voyage,
But jealousy what might befall your travel,
Being skilless in these parts; which to a stranger,
Unguided and unfriended, often prove
Rough and unhospitable: my willing love,
The rather by these arguments of fear,
Set forth in your pursuit.

speeches.speech: My kind Antonio, I can no other answer make but thanks, And thanks; and ever thanks; and oft good turns Are shuffled off with such uncurrent pay: But, were my worth as is my conscience firm, You should find better dealing. What's to do? Shall we go see the reliques of this town?
lines: My kind Antonio,
I can no other answer make but thanks,
And thanks; and ever thanks; and oft good turns
Are shuffled off with such uncurrent pay:
But, were my worth as is my conscience firm,
You should find better dealing. What's to do?
Shall we go see the reliques of this town?

speeches.speech: To-morrow, sir: best first go see your lodging.
lines: To-morrow, sir: best first go see your lodging.

speeches.speech: I am not weary, and 'tis long to night: I pray you, let us satisfy our eyes With the memorials and the things of fame That do renown this city.
lines: I am not weary, and 'tis long to night:
I pray you, let us satisfy our eyes
With the memorials and the things of fame
That do renown this city.

speeches.speech: Would you'ld pardon me; I do not without danger walk these streets: Once, in a sea-fight, 'gainst the count his galleys I did some service; of such note indeed, That were I ta'en here it would scarce be answer'd.
lines: Would you'ld pardon me;
I do not without danger walk these streets:
Once, in a sea-fight, 'gainst the count his galleys
I did some service; of such note indeed,
That were I ta'en here it would scarce be answer'd.

speeches.speech: Belike you slew great number of his people.
lines: Belike you slew great number of his people.

speeches.speech: The offence is not of such a bloody nature; Albeit the quality of the time and quarrel Might well have given us bloody argument. It might have since been answer'd in repaying What we took from them; which, for traffic's sake, Most of our city did: only myself stood out; For which, if I be lapsed in this place, I shall pay dear.
lines: The offence is not of such a bloody nature;
Albeit the quality of the time and quarrel
Might well have given us bloody argument.
It might have since been answer'd in repaying
What we took from them; which, for traffic's sake,
Most of our city did: only myself stood out;
For which, if I be lapsed in this place,
I shall pay dear.

speeches.speech: Do not then walk too open.
lines: Do not then walk too open.

speeches.speech: It doth not fit me. Hold, sir, here's my purse. In the south suburbs, at the Elephant, Is best to lodge: I will bespeak our diet, Whiles you beguile the time and feed your knowledge With viewing of the town: there shall you have me.
lines: It doth not fit me. Hold, sir, here's my purse.
In the south suburbs, at the Elephant,
Is best to lodge: I will bespeak our diet,
Whiles you beguile the time and feed your knowledge
With viewing of the town: there shall you have me.

speeches.speech: Why I your purse?
lines: Why I your purse?

speeches.speech: Haply your eye shall light upon some toy You have desire to purchase; and your store, I think, is not for idle markets, sir.
lines: Haply your eye shall light upon some toy
You have desire to purchase; and your store,
I think, is not for idle markets, sir.

speeches.speech: I'll be your purse-bearer and leave you For an hour.
lines: I'll be your purse-bearer and leave you
For an hour.

speeches.speech: To the Elephant.
lines: To the Elephant.

speeches.speech: I do remember.
lines: I do remember.

speeches.speech: I have sent after him: he says he'll come; How shall I feast him? what bestow of him? For youth is bought more oft than begg'd or borrow'd. I speak too loud. Where is Malvolio? he is sad and civil, And suits well for a servant with my fortunes: Where is Malvolio?
lines: I have sent after him: he says he'll come;
How shall I feast him? what bestow of him?
For youth is bought more oft than begg'd or borrow'd.
I speak too loud.
Where is Malvolio? he is sad and civil,
And suits well for a servant with my fortunes:
Where is Malvolio?

speeches.speech: He's coming, madam; but in very strange manner. He is, sure, possessed, madam.
lines: He's coming, madam; but in very strange manner. He
is, sure, possessed, madam.

speeches.speech: Why, what's the matter? does he rave?
lines: Why, what's the matter? does he rave?

speeches.speech: No. madam, he does nothing but smile: your ladyship were best to have some guard about you, if he come; for, sure, the man is tainted in's wits.
lines: No. madam, he does nothing but smile: your
ladyship were best to have some guard about you, if
he come; for, sure, the man is tainted in's wits.

speeches.speech: Go call him hither. [Exit MARIA] I am as mad as he, If sad and merry madness equal be. [Re-enter MARIA, with MALVOLIO] How now, Malvolio!
lines: Go call him hither.
[Exit MARIA]
I am as mad as he,
If sad and merry madness equal be.
[Re-enter MARIA, with MALVOLIO]
How now, Malvolio!

speeches.speech: Sweet lady, ho, ho.
lines: Sweet lady, ho, ho.

speeches.speech: Smilest thou? I sent for thee upon a sad occasion.
lines: Smilest thou?
I sent for thee upon a sad occasion.

speeches.speech: Sad, lady! I could be sad: this does make some obstruction in the blood, this cross-gartering; but what of that? if it please the eye of one, it is with me as the very true sonnet is, 'Please one, and please all.'
lines: Sad, lady! I could be sad: this does make some
obstruction in the blood, this cross-gartering; but
what of that? if it please the eye of one, it is
with me as the very true sonnet is, 'Please one, and
please all.'

speeches.speech: Why, how dost thou, man? what is the matter with thee?
lines: Why, how dost thou, man? what is the matter with thee?

speeches.speech: Not black in my mind, though yellow in my legs. It did come to his hands, and commands shall be executed: I think we do know the sweet Roman hand.
lines: Not black in my mind, though yellow in my legs. It
did come to his hands, and commands shall be
executed: I think we do know the sweet Roman hand.

speeches.speech: Wilt thou go to bed, Malvolio?
lines: Wilt thou go to bed, Malvolio?

speeches.speech: To bed! ay, sweet-heart, and I'll come to thee.
lines: To bed! ay, sweet-heart, and I'll come to thee.

speeches.speech: God comfort thee! Why dost thou smile so and kiss thy hand so oft?
lines: God comfort thee! Why dost thou smile so and kiss
thy hand so oft?

speeches.speech: How do you, Malvolio?
lines: How do you, Malvolio?

speeches.speech: At your request! yes; nightingales answer daws.
lines: At your request! yes; nightingales answer daws.

speeches.speech: Why appear you with this ridiculous boldness before my lady?
lines: Why appear you with this ridiculous boldness before my lady?

speeches.speech: 'Be not afraid of greatness:' 'twas well writ.
lines: 'Be not afraid of greatness:' 'twas well writ.

speeches.speech: What meanest thou by that, Malvolio?
lines: What meanest thou by that, Malvolio?

speeches.speech: 'Some are born great,'--
lines: 'Some are born great,'--

speeches.speech: Ha!
lines: Ha!

speeches.speech: 'Some achieve greatness,'--
lines: 'Some achieve greatness,'--

speeches.speech: What sayest thou?
lines: What sayest thou?

speeches.speech: 'And some have greatness thrust upon them.'
lines: 'And some have greatness thrust upon them.'

speeches.speech: Heaven restore thee!
lines: Heaven restore thee!

speeches.speech: 'Remember who commended thy yellow stockings,'--
lines: 'Remember who commended thy yellow stockings,'--

speeches.speech: Thy yellow stockings!
lines: Thy yellow stockings!

speeches.speech: 'And wished to see thee cross-gartered.'
lines: 'And wished to see thee cross-gartered.'

speeches.speech: Cross-gartered!
lines: Cross-gartered!

speeches.speech: 'Go to thou art made, if thou desirest to be so;'--
lines: 'Go to thou art made, if thou desirest to be so;'--

speeches.speech: Am I made?
lines: Am I made?

speeches.speech: 'If not, let me see thee a servant still.'
lines: 'If not, let me see thee a servant still.'

speeches.speech: Why, this is very midsummer madness.
lines: Why, this is very midsummer madness.

speeches.speech: Madam, the young gentleman of the Count Orsino's is returned: I could hardly entreat him back: he attends your ladyship's pleasure.
lines: Madam, the young gentleman of the Count Orsino's is
returned: I could hardly entreat him back: he
attends your ladyship's pleasure.

speeches.speech: I'll come to him. [Exit Servant] Good Maria, let this fellow be looked to. Where's my cousin Toby? Let some of my people have a special care of him: I would not have him miscarry for the half of my dowry.
lines: I'll come to him.
[Exit Servant]
Good Maria, let this fellow be looked to. Where's
my cousin Toby? Let some of my people have a special
care of him: I would not have him miscarry for the
half of my dowry.

speeches.speech: O, ho! do you come near me now? no worse man than Sir Toby to look to me! This concurs directly with the letter: she sends him on purpose, that I may appear stubborn to him; for she incites me to that in the letter. 'Cast thy humble slough,' says she; 'be opposite with a kinsman, surly with servants; let thy tongue tang with arguments of state; put thyself into the trick of singularity;' and consequently sets down the manner how; as, a sad face, a reverend carriage, a slow tongue, in the habit of some sir of note, and so forth. I have limed her; but it is Jove's doing, and Jove make me thankful! And when she went away now, 'Let this fellow be looked to:' fellow! not Malvolio, nor after my degree, but fellow. Why, every thing adheres together, that no dram of a scruple, no scruple of a scruple, no obstacle, no incredulous or unsafe circumstance--What can be said? Nothing that can be can come between me and the full prospect of my hopes. Well, Jove, not I, is the doer of this, and he is to be thanked.
lines: O, ho! do you come near me now? no worse man than
Sir Toby to look to me! This concurs directly with
the letter: she sends him on purpose, that I may
appear stubborn to him; for she incites me to that
in the letter. 'Cast thy humble slough,' says she;
'be opposite with a kinsman, surly with servants;
let thy tongue tang with arguments of state; put
thyself into the trick of singularity;' and
consequently sets down the manner how; as, a sad
face, a reverend carriage, a slow tongue, in the
habit of some sir of note, and so forth. I have
limed her; but it is Jove's doing, and Jove make me
thankful! And when she went away now, 'Let this
fellow be looked to:' fellow! not Malvolio, nor
after my degree, but fellow. Why, every thing
adheres together, that no dram of a scruple, no
scruple of a scruple, no obstacle, no incredulous
or unsafe circumstance--What can be said? Nothing
that can be can come between me and the full
prospect of my hopes. Well, Jove, not I, is the
doer of this, and he is to be thanked.

speeches.speech: Which way is he, in the name of sanctity? If all the devils of hell be drawn in little, and Legion himself possessed him, yet I'll speak to him.
lines: Which way is he, in the name of sanctity? If all
the devils of hell be drawn in little, and Legion
himself possessed him, yet I'll speak to him.

speeches.speech: Here he is, here he is. How is't with you, sir? how is't with you, man?
lines: Here he is, here he is. How is't with you, sir?
how is't with you, man?

speeches.speech: Go off; I discard you: let me enjoy my private: go off.
lines: Go off; I discard you: let me enjoy my private: go
off.

speeches.speech: Lo, how hollow the fiend speaks within him! did not I tell you? Sir Toby, my lady prays you to have a care of him.
lines: Lo, how hollow the fiend speaks within him! did not
I tell you? Sir Toby, my lady prays you to have a
care of him.

speeches.speech: Ah, ha! does she so?
lines: Ah, ha! does she so?

speeches.speech: Go to, go to; peace, peace; we must deal gently with him: let me alone. How do you, Malvolio? how is't with you? What, man! defy the devil: consider, he's an enemy to mankind.
lines: Go to, go to; peace, peace; we must deal gently
with him: let me alone. How do you, Malvolio? how
is't with you? What, man! defy the devil:
consider, he's an enemy to mankind.

speeches.speech: Do you know what you say?
lines: Do you know what you say?

speeches.speech: La you, an you speak ill of the devil, how he takes it at heart! Pray God, he be not bewitched!
lines: La you, an you speak ill of the devil, how he takes
it at heart! Pray God, he be not bewitched!

speeches.speech: Carry his water to the wise woman.
lines: Carry his water to the wise woman.

speeches.speech: Marry, and it shall be done to-morrow morning, if I live. My lady would not lose him for more than I'll say.
lines: Marry, and it shall be done to-morrow morning, if I
live. My lady would not lose him for more than I'll say.

speeches.speech: How now, mistress!
lines: How now, mistress!

speeches.speech: O Lord!
lines: O Lord!

speeches.speech: Prithee, hold thy peace; this is not the way: do you not see you move him? let me alone with him.
lines: Prithee, hold thy peace; this is not the way: do
you not see you move him? let me alone with him.

speeches.speech: No way but gentleness; gently, gently: the fiend is rough, and will not be roughly used.
lines: No way but gentleness; gently, gently: the fiend is
rough, and will not be roughly used.

speeches.speech: Why, how now, my bawcock! how dost thou, chuck?
lines: Why, how now, my bawcock! how dost thou, chuck?

speeches.speech: Sir!
lines: Sir!

speeches.speech: Ay, Biddy, come with me. What, man! 'tis not for gravity to play at cherry-pit with Satan: hang him, foul collier!
lines: Ay, Biddy, come with me. What, man! 'tis not for
gravity to play at cherry-pit with Satan: hang
him, foul collier!

speeches.speech: Get him to say his prayers, good Sir Toby, get him to pray.
lines: Get him to say his prayers, good Sir Toby, get him to pray.

speeches.speech: My prayers, minx!
lines: My prayers, minx!

speeches.speech: No, I warrant you, he will not hear of godliness.
lines: No, I warrant you, he will not hear of godliness.

speeches.speech: Go, hang yourselves all! you are idle shallow things: I am not of your element: you shall know more hereafter.
lines: Go, hang yourselves all! you are idle shallow
things: I am not of your element: you shall know
more hereafter.

speeches.speech: Is't possible?
lines: Is't possible?

speeches.speech: If this were played upon a stage now, I could condemn it as an improbable fiction.
lines: If this were played upon a stage now, I could
condemn it as an improbable fiction.

speeches.speech: His very genius hath taken the infection of the device, man.
lines: His very genius hath taken the infection of the device, man.

speeches.speech: Nay, pursue him now, lest the device take air and taint.
lines: Nay, pursue him now, lest the device take air and taint.

speeches.speech: Why, we shall make him mad indeed.
lines: Why, we shall make him mad indeed.

speeches.speech: The house will be the quieter.
lines: The house will be the quieter.

speeches.speech: Come, we'll have him in a dark room and bound. My niece is already in the belief that he's mad: we may carry it thus, for our pleasure and his penance, till our very pastime, tired out of breath, prompt us to have mercy on him: at which time we will bring the device to the bar and crown thee for a finder of madmen. But see, but see.
lines: Come, we'll have him in a dark room and bound. My
niece is already in the belief that he's mad: we
may carry it thus, for our pleasure and his penance,
till our very pastime, tired out of breath, prompt
us to have mercy on him: at which time we will
bring the device to the bar and crown thee for a
finder of madmen. But see, but see.

speeches.speech: More matter for a May morning.
lines: More matter for a May morning.

speeches.speech: Here's the challenge, read it: warrant there's vinegar and pepper in't.
lines: Here's the challenge, read it: warrant there's
vinegar and pepper in't.

speeches.speech: Is't so saucy?
lines: Is't so saucy?

speeches.speech: Ay, is't, I warrant him: do but read.
lines: Ay, is't, I warrant him: do but read.

speeches.speech: Give me. [Reads] 'Youth, whatsoever thou art, thou art but a scurvy fellow.'
lines: Give me.
[Reads]
'Youth, whatsoever thou art, thou art but a scurvy fellow.'

speeches.speech: Good, and valiant.
lines: Good, and valiant.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'Wonder not, nor admire not in thy mind, why I do call thee so, for I will show thee no reason for't.'
lines: [Reads] 'Wonder not, nor admire not in thy mind,
why I do call thee so, for I will show thee no reason for't.'

speeches.speech: A good note; that keeps you from the blow of the law.
lines: A good note; that keeps you from the blow of the law.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'Thou comest to the lady Olivia, and in my sight she uses thee kindly: but thou liest in thy throat; that is not the matter I challenge thee for.'
lines: [Reads] 'Thou comest to the lady Olivia, and in my
sight she uses thee kindly: but thou liest in thy
throat; that is not the matter I challenge thee for.'

speeches.speech: Very brief, and to exceeding good sense--less.
lines: Very brief, and to exceeding good sense--less.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'I will waylay thee going home; where if it be thy chance to kill me,'--
lines: [Reads] 'I will waylay thee going home; where if it
be thy chance to kill me,'--

speeches.speech: Good.
lines: Good.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'Thou killest me like a rogue and a villain.'
lines: [Reads] 'Thou killest me like a rogue and a villain.'

speeches.speech: Still you keep o' the windy side of the law: good.
lines: Still you keep o' the windy side of the law: good.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'Fare thee well; and God have mercy upon one of our souls! He may have mercy upon mine; but my hope is better, and so look to thyself. Thy friend, as thou usest him, and thy sworn enemy, ANDREW AGUECHEEK. If this letter move him not, his legs cannot: I'll give't him.
lines: [Reads] 'Fare thee well; and God have mercy upon
one of our souls! He may have mercy upon mine; but
my hope is better, and so look to thyself. Thy
friend, as thou usest him, and thy sworn enemy,
ANDREW AGUECHEEK.
If this letter move him not, his legs cannot:
I'll give't him.

speeches.speech: You may have very fit occasion for't: he is now in some commerce with my lady, and will by and by depart.
lines: You may have very fit occasion for't: he is now in
some commerce with my lady, and will by and by depart.

speeches.speech: Go, Sir Andrew: scout me for him at the corner the orchard like a bum-baily: so soon as ever thou seest him, draw; and, as thou drawest swear horrible; for it comes to pass oft that a terrible oath, with a swaggering accent sharply twanged off, gives manhood more approbation than ever proof itself would have earned him. Away!
lines: Go, Sir Andrew: scout me for him at the corner the
orchard like a bum-baily: so soon as ever thou seest
him, draw; and, as thou drawest swear horrible; for
it comes to pass oft that a terrible oath, with a
swaggering accent sharply twanged off, gives manhood
more approbation than ever proof itself would have
earned him. Away!

speeches.speech: Nay, let me alone for swearing.
lines: Nay, let me alone for swearing.

speeches.speech: Now will not I deliver his letter: for the behavior of the young gentleman gives him out to be of good capacity and breeding; his employment between his lord and my niece confirms no less: therefore this letter, being so excellently ignorant, will breed no terror in the youth: he will find it comes from a clodpole. But, sir, I will deliver his challenge by word of mouth; set upon Aguecheek a notable report of valour; and drive the gentleman, as I know his youth will aptly receive it, into a most hideous opinion of his rage, skill, fury and impetuosity. This will so fright them both that they will kill one another by the look, like cockatrices.
lines: Now will not I deliver his letter: for the behavior
of the young gentleman gives him out to be of good
capacity and breeding; his employment between his
lord and my niece confirms no less: therefore this
letter, being so excellently ignorant, will breed no
terror in the youth: he will find it comes from a
clodpole. But, sir, I will deliver his challenge by
word of mouth; set upon Aguecheek a notable report
of valour; and drive the gentleman, as I know his
youth will aptly receive it, into a most hideous
opinion of his rage, skill, fury and impetuosity.
This will so fright them both that they will kill
one another by the look, like cockatrices.

speeches.speech: Here he comes with your niece: give them way till he take leave, and presently after him.
lines: Here he comes with your niece: give them way till
he take leave, and presently after him.

speeches.speech: I will meditate the while upon some horrid message for a challenge.
lines: I will meditate the while upon some horrid message
for a challenge.

speeches.speech: I have said too much unto a heart of stone And laid mine honour too unchary out: There's something in me that reproves my fault; But such a headstrong potent fault it is, That it but mocks reproof.
lines: I have said too much unto a heart of stone
And laid mine honour too unchary out:
There's something in me that reproves my fault;
But such a headstrong potent fault it is,
That it but mocks reproof.

speeches.speech: With the same 'havior that your passion bears Goes on my master's grief.
lines: With the same 'havior that your passion bears
Goes on my master's grief.

speeches.speech: Here, wear this jewel for me, 'tis my picture; Refuse it not; it hath no tongue to vex you; And I beseech you come again to-morrow. What shall you ask of me that I'll deny, That honour saved may upon asking give?
lines: Here, wear this jewel for me, 'tis my picture;
Refuse it not; it hath no tongue to vex you;
And I beseech you come again to-morrow.
What shall you ask of me that I'll deny,
That honour saved may upon asking give?

speeches.speech: Nothing but this; your true love for my master.
lines: Nothing but this; your true love for my master.

speeches.speech: How with mine honour may I give him that Which I have given to you?
lines: How with mine honour may I give him that
Which I have given to you?

speeches.speech: I will acquit you.
lines: I will acquit you.

speeches.speech: Well, come again to-morrow: fare thee well: A fiend like thee might bear my soul to hell.
lines: Well, come again to-morrow: fare thee well:
A fiend like thee might bear my soul to hell.

speeches.speech: Gentleman, God save thee.
lines: Gentleman, God save thee.

speeches.speech: And you, sir.
lines: And you, sir.

speeches.speech: That defence thou hast, betake thee to't: of what nature the wrongs are thou hast done him, I know not; but thy intercepter, full of despite, bloody as the hunter, attends thee at the orchard-end: dismount thy tuck, be yare in thy preparation, for thy assailant is quick, skilful and deadly.
lines: That defence thou hast, betake thee to't: of what
nature the wrongs are thou hast done him, I know
not; but thy intercepter, full of despite, bloody as
the hunter, attends thee at the orchard-end:
dismount thy tuck, be yare in thy preparation, for
thy assailant is quick, skilful and deadly.

speeches.speech: You mistake, sir; I am sure no man hath any quarrel to me: my remembrance is very free and clear from any image of offence done to any man.
lines: You mistake, sir; I am sure no man hath any quarrel
to me: my remembrance is very free and clear from
any image of offence done to any man.

speeches.speech: You'll find it otherwise, I assure you: therefore, if you hold your life at any price, betake you to your guard; for your opposite hath in him what youth, strength, skill and wrath can furnish man withal.
lines: You'll find it otherwise, I assure you: therefore,
if you hold your life at any price, betake you to
your guard; for your opposite hath in him what
youth, strength, skill and wrath can furnish man withal.

speeches.speech: I pray you, sir, what is he?
lines: I pray you, sir, what is he?

speeches.speech: He is knight, dubbed with unhatched rapier and on carpet consideration; but he is a devil in private brawl: souls and bodies hath he divorced three; and his incensement at this moment is so implacable, that satisfaction can be none but by pangs of death and sepulchre. Hob, nob, is his word; give't or take't.
lines: He is knight, dubbed with unhatched rapier and on
carpet consideration; but he is a devil in private
brawl: souls and bodies hath he divorced three; and
his incensement at this moment is so implacable,
that satisfaction can be none but by pangs of death
and sepulchre. Hob, nob, is his word; give't or take't.

speeches.speech: I will return again into the house and desire some conduct of the lady. I am no fighter. I have heard of some kind of men that put quarrels purposely on others, to taste their valour: belike this is a man of that quirk.
lines: I will return again into the house and desire some
conduct of the lady. I am no fighter. I have heard
of some kind of men that put quarrels purposely on
others, to taste their valour: belike this is a man
of that quirk.

speeches.speech: Sir, no; his indignation derives itself out of a very competent injury: therefore, get you on and give him his desire. Back you shall not to the house, unless you undertake that with me which with as much safety you might answer him: therefore, on, or strip your sword stark naked; for meddle you must, that's certain, or forswear to wear iron about you.
lines: Sir, no; his indignation derives itself out of a
very competent injury: therefore, get you on and
give him his desire. Back you shall not to the
house, unless you undertake that with me which with
as much safety you might answer him: therefore, on,
or strip your sword stark naked; for meddle you
must, that's certain, or forswear to wear iron about you.

speeches.speech: This is as uncivil as strange. I beseech you, do me this courteous office, as to know of the knight what my offence to him is: it is something of my negligence, nothing of my purpose.
lines: This is as uncivil as strange. I beseech you, do me
this courteous office, as to know of the knight what
my offence to him is: it is something of my
negligence, nothing of my purpose.

speeches.speech: I will do so. Signior Fabian, stay you by this gentleman till my return.
lines: I will do so. Signior Fabian, stay you by this
gentleman till my return.

speeches.speech: Pray you, sir, do you know of this matter?
lines: Pray you, sir, do you know of this matter?

speeches.speech: I know the knight is incensed against you, even to a mortal arbitrement; but nothing of the circumstance more.
lines: I know the knight is incensed against you, even to a
mortal arbitrement; but nothing of the circumstance more.

speeches.speech: I beseech you, what manner of man is he?
lines: I beseech you, what manner of man is he?

speeches.speech: Nothing of that wonderful promise, to read him by his form, as you are like to find him in the proof of his valour. He is, indeed, sir, the most skilful, bloody and fatal opposite that you could possibly have found in any part of Illyria. Will you walk towards him? I will make your peace with him if I can.
lines: Nothing of that wonderful promise, to read him by
his form, as you are like to find him in the proof
of his valour. He is, indeed, sir, the most skilful,
bloody and fatal opposite that you could possibly
have found in any part of Illyria. Will you walk
towards him? I will make your peace with him if I
can.

speeches.speech: I shall be much bound to you for't: I am one that had rather go with sir priest than sir knight: I care not who knows so much of my mettle.
lines: I shall be much bound to you for't: I am one that
had rather go with sir priest than sir knight: I
care not who knows so much of my mettle.

speeches.speech: Why, man, he's a very devil; I have not seen such a firago. I had a pass with him, rapier, scabbard and all, and he gives me the stuck in with such a mortal motion, that it is inevitable; and on the answer, he pays you as surely as your feet hit the ground they step on. They say he has been fencer to the Sophy.
lines: Why, man, he's a very devil; I have not seen such a
firago. I had a pass with him, rapier, scabbard and
all, and he gives me the stuck in with such a mortal
motion, that it is inevitable; and on the answer, he
pays you as surely as your feet hit the ground they
step on. They say he has been fencer to the Sophy.

speeches.speech: Pox on't, I'll not meddle with him.
lines: Pox on't, I'll not meddle with him.

speeches.speech: Ay, but he will not now be pacified: Fabian can scarce hold him yonder.
lines: Ay, but he will not now be pacified: Fabian can
scarce hold him yonder.

speeches.speech: Plague on't, an I thought he had been valiant and so cunning in fence, I'ld have seen him damned ere I'ld have challenged him. Let him let the matter slip, and I'll give him my horse, grey Capilet.
lines: Plague on't, an I thought he had been valiant and so
cunning in fence, I'ld have seen him damned ere I'ld
have challenged him. Let him let the matter slip,
and I'll give him my horse, grey Capilet.

speeches.speech: I'll make the motion: stand here, make a good show on't: this shall end without the perdition of souls. [Aside] Marry, I'll ride your horse as well as I ride you. [Re-enter FABIAN and VIOLA] [To FABIAN] I have his horse to take up the quarrel: I have persuaded him the youth's a devil.
lines: I'll make the motion: stand here, make a good show
on't: this shall end without the perdition of souls.
[Aside]
Marry, I'll ride your horse as well as I ride you.
[Re-enter FABIAN and VIOLA]
[To FABIAN]
I have his horse to take up the quarrel:
I have persuaded him the youth's a devil.

speeches.speech: He is as horribly conceited of him; and pants and looks pale, as if a bear were at his heels.
lines: He is as horribly conceited of him; and pants and
looks pale, as if a bear were at his heels.

speeches.speech: [To VIOLA] There's no remedy, sir; he will fight with you for's oath sake: marry, he hath better bethought him of his quarrel, and he finds that now scarce to be worth talking of: therefore draw, for the supportance of his vow; he protests he will not hurt you.
lines: [To VIOLA] There's no remedy, sir; he will fight
with you for's oath sake: marry, he hath better
bethought him of his quarrel, and he finds that now
scarce to be worth talking of: therefore draw, for
the supportance of his vow; he protests he will not hurt you.

speeches.speech: [Aside] Pray God defend me! A little thing would make me tell them how much I lack of a man.
lines: [Aside] Pray God defend me! A little thing would
make me tell them how much I lack of a man.

speeches.speech: Give ground, if you see him furious.
lines: Give ground, if you see him furious.

speeches.speech: Come, Sir Andrew, there's no remedy; the gentleman will, for his honour's sake, have one bout with you; he cannot by the duello avoid it: but he has promised me, as he is a gentleman and a soldier, he will not hurt you. Come on; to't.
lines: Come, Sir Andrew, there's no remedy; the gentleman
will, for his honour's sake, have one bout with you;
he cannot by the duello avoid it: but he has
promised me, as he is a gentleman and a soldier, he
will not hurt you. Come on; to't.

speeches.speech: Pray God, he keep his oath!
lines: Pray God, he keep his oath!

speeches.speech: I do assure you, 'tis against my will.
lines: I do assure you, 'tis against my will.

speeches.speech: Put up your sword. If this young gentleman Have done offence, I take the fault on me: If you offend him, I for him defy you.
