lines: Put up your sword. If this young gentleman
Have done offence, I take the fault on me:
If you offend him, I for him defy you.

speeches.speech: You, sir! why, what are you?
lines: You, sir! why, what are you?

speeches.speech: One, sir, that for his love dares yet do more Than you have heard him brag to you he will.
lines: One, sir, that for his love dares yet do more
Than you have heard him brag to you he will.

speeches.speech: Nay, if you be an undertaker, I am for you.
lines: Nay, if you be an undertaker, I am for you.

speeches.speech: O good Sir Toby, hold! here come the officers.
lines: O good Sir Toby, hold! here come the officers.

speeches.speech: I'll be with you anon.
lines: I'll be with you anon.

speeches.speech: Pray, sir, put your sword up, if you please.
lines: Pray, sir, put your sword up, if you please.

speeches.speech: Marry, will I, sir; and, for that I promised you, I'll be as good as my word: he will bear you easily and reins well.
lines: Marry, will I, sir; and, for that I promised you,
I'll be as good as my word: he will bear you easily
and reins well.

speeches.speech: This is the man; do thy office.
lines: This is the man; do thy office.

speeches.speech: Antonio, I arrest thee at the suit of Count Orsino.
lines: Antonio, I arrest thee at the suit of Count Orsino.

speeches.speech: You do mistake me, sir.
lines: You do mistake me, sir.

speeches.speech: No, sir, no jot; I know your favour well, Though now you have no sea-cap on your head. Take him away: he knows I know him well.
lines: No, sir, no jot; I know your favour well,
Though now you have no sea-cap on your head.
Take him away: he knows I know him well.

speeches.speech: I must obey. [To VIOLA] This comes with seeking you: But there's no remedy; I shall answer it. What will you do, now my necessity Makes me to ask you for my purse? It grieves me Much more for what I cannot do for you Than what befalls myself. You stand amazed; But be of comfort.
lines: I must obey.
[To VIOLA]
This comes with seeking you:
But there's no remedy; I shall answer it.
What will you do, now my necessity
Makes me to ask you for my purse? It grieves me
Much more for what I cannot do for you
Than what befalls myself. You stand amazed;
But be of comfort.

speeches.speech: Come, sir, away.
lines: Come, sir, away.

speeches.speech: I must entreat of you some of that money.
lines: I must entreat of you some of that money.

speeches.speech: What money, sir? For the fair kindness you have show'd me here, And, part, being prompted by your present trouble, Out of my lean and low ability I'll lend you something: my having is not much; I'll make division of my present with you: Hold, there's half my coffer.
lines: What money, sir?
For the fair kindness you have show'd me here,
And, part, being prompted by your present trouble,
Out of my lean and low ability
I'll lend you something: my having is not much;
I'll make division of my present with you:
Hold, there's half my coffer.

speeches.speech: Will you deny me now? Is't possible that my deserts to you Can lack persuasion? Do not tempt my misery, Lest that it make me so unsound a man As to upbraid you with those kindnesses That I have done for you.
lines: Will you deny me now?
Is't possible that my deserts to you
Can lack persuasion? Do not tempt my misery,
Lest that it make me so unsound a man
As to upbraid you with those kindnesses
That I have done for you.

speeches.speech: I know of none; Nor know I you by voice or any feature: I hate ingratitude more in a man Than lying, vainness, babbling, drunkenness, Or any taint of vice whose strong corruption Inhabits our frail blood.
lines: I know of none;
Nor know I you by voice or any feature:
I hate ingratitude more in a man
Than lying, vainness, babbling, drunkenness,
Or any taint of vice whose strong corruption
Inhabits our frail blood.

speeches.speech: O heavens themselves!
lines: O heavens themselves!

speeches.speech: Come, sir, I pray you, go.
lines: Come, sir, I pray you, go.

speeches.speech: Let me speak a little. This youth that you see here I snatch'd one half out of the jaws of death, Relieved him with such sanctity of love, And to his image, which methought did promise Most venerable worth, did I devotion.
lines: Let me speak a little. This youth that you see here
I snatch'd one half out of the jaws of death,
Relieved him with such sanctity of love,
And to his image, which methought did promise
Most venerable worth, did I devotion.

speeches.speech: What's that to us? The time goes by: away!
lines: What's that to us? The time goes by: away!

speeches.speech: But O how vile an idol proves this god Thou hast, Sebastian, done good feature shame. In nature there's no blemish but the mind; None can be call'd deform'd but the unkind: Virtue is beauty, but the beauteous evil Are empty trunks o'erflourish'd by the devil.
lines: But O how vile an idol proves this god
Thou hast, Sebastian, done good feature shame.
In nature there's no blemish but the mind;
None can be call'd deform'd but the unkind:
Virtue is beauty, but the beauteous evil
Are empty trunks o'erflourish'd by the devil.

speeches.speech: The man grows mad: away with him! Come, come, sir.
lines: The man grows mad: away with him! Come, come, sir.

speeches.speech: Lead me on.
lines: Lead me on.

speeches.speech: Methinks his words do from such passion fly, That he believes himself: so do not I. Prove true, imagination, O, prove true, That I, dear brother, be now ta'en for you!
lines: Methinks his words do from such passion fly,
That he believes himself: so do not I.
Prove true, imagination, O, prove true,
That I, dear brother, be now ta'en for you!

speeches.speech: Come hither, knight; come hither, Fabian: we'll whisper o'er a couplet or two of most sage saws.
lines: Come hither, knight; come hither, Fabian: we'll
whisper o'er a couplet or two of most sage saws.

speeches.speech: He named Sebastian: I my brother know Yet living in my glass; even such and so In favour was my brother, and he went Still in this fashion, colour, ornament, For him I imitate: O, if it prove, Tempests are kind and salt waves fresh in love.
lines: He named Sebastian: I my brother know
Yet living in my glass; even such and so
In favour was my brother, and he went
Still in this fashion, colour, ornament,
For him I imitate: O, if it prove,
Tempests are kind and salt waves fresh in love.

speeches.speech: A very dishonest paltry boy, and more a coward than a hare: his dishonesty appears in leaving his friend here in necessity and denying him; and for his cowardship, ask Fabian.
lines: A very dishonest paltry boy, and more a coward than
a hare: his dishonesty appears in leaving his
friend here in necessity and denying him; and for
his cowardship, ask Fabian.

speeches.speech: A coward, a most devout coward, religious in it.
lines: A coward, a most devout coward, religious in it.

speeches.speech: 'Slid, I'll after him again and beat him.
lines: 'Slid, I'll after him again and beat him.

speeches.speech: Do; cuff him soundly, but never draw thy sword.
lines: Do; cuff him soundly, but never draw thy sword.

speeches.speech: An I do not,--
lines: An I do not,--

speeches.speech: Come, let's see the event.
lines: Come, let's see the event.

speeches.speech: I dare lay any money 'twill be nothing yet.
lines: I dare lay any money 'twill be nothing yet.

speeches.speech: Will you make me believe that I am not sent for you?
lines: Will you make me believe that I am not sent for you?

speeches.speech: Go to, go to, thou art a foolish fellow: Let me be clear of thee.
lines: Go to, go to, thou art a foolish fellow:
Let me be clear of thee.

speeches.speech: Well held out, i' faith! No, I do not know you; nor I am not sent to you by my lady, to bid you come speak with her; nor your name is not Master Cesario; nor this is not my nose neither. Nothing that is so is so.
lines: Well held out, i' faith! No, I do not know you; nor
I am not sent to you by my lady, to bid you come
speak with her; nor your name is not Master Cesario;
nor this is not my nose neither. Nothing that is so is so.

speeches.speech: I prithee, vent thy folly somewhere else: Thou know'st not me.
lines: I prithee, vent thy folly somewhere else: Thou
know'st not me.

speeches.speech: Vent my folly! he has heard that word of some great man and now applies it to a fool. Vent my folly! I am afraid this great lubber, the world, will prove a cockney. I prithee now, ungird thy strangeness and tell me what I shall vent to my lady: shall I vent to her that thou art coming?
lines: Vent my folly! he has heard that word of some
great man and now applies it to a fool. Vent my
folly! I am afraid this great lubber, the world,
will prove a cockney. I prithee now, ungird thy
strangeness and tell me what I shall vent to my
lady: shall I vent to her that thou art coming?

speeches.speech: I prithee, foolish Greek, depart from me: There's money for thee: if you tarry longer, I shall give worse payment.
lines: I prithee, foolish Greek, depart from me: There's
money for thee: if you tarry longer, I shall give
worse payment.

speeches.speech: By my troth, thou hast an open hand. These wise men that give fools money get themselves a good report--after fourteen years' purchase.
lines: By my troth, thou hast an open hand. These wise men
that give fools money get themselves a good
report--after fourteen years' purchase.

speeches.speech: Now, sir, have I met you again? there's for you.
lines: Now, sir, have I met you again? there's for you.

speeches.speech: Why, there's for thee, and there, and there. Are all the people mad?
lines: Why, there's for thee, and there, and there. Are all
the people mad?

speeches.speech: Hold, sir, or I'll throw your dagger o'er the house.
lines: Hold, sir, or I'll throw your dagger o'er the house.

speeches.speech: This will I tell my lady straight: I would not be in some of your coats for two pence.
lines: This will I tell my lady straight: I would not be
in some of your coats for two pence.

speeches.speech: Come on, sir; hold.
lines: Come on, sir; hold.

speeches.speech: Nay, let him alone: I'll go another way to work with him; I'll have an action of battery against him, if there be any law in Illyria: though I struck him first, yet it's no matter for that.
lines: Nay, let him alone: I'll go another way to work
with him; I'll have an action of battery against
him, if there be any law in Illyria: though I
struck him first, yet it's no matter for that.

speeches.speech: Let go thy hand.
lines: Let go thy hand.

speeches.speech: Come, sir, I will not let you go. Come, my young soldier, put up your iron: you are well fleshed; come on.
lines: Come, sir, I will not let you go. Come, my young
soldier, put up your iron: you are well fleshed; come on.

speeches.speech: I will be free from thee. What wouldst thou now? If thou darest tempt me further, draw thy sword.
lines: I will be free from thee. What wouldst thou now? If
thou darest tempt me further, draw thy sword.

speeches.speech: What, what? Nay, then I must have an ounce or two of this malapert blood from you.
lines: What, what? Nay, then I must have an ounce or two
of this malapert blood from you.

speeches.speech: Hold, Toby; on thy life I charge thee, hold!
lines: Hold, Toby; on thy life I charge thee, hold!

speeches.speech: Madam!
lines: Madam!

speeches.speech: Will it be ever thus? Ungracious wretch, Fit for the mountains and the barbarous caves, Where manners ne'er were preach'd! out of my sight! Be not offended, dear Cesario. Rudesby, be gone! [Exeunt SIR TOBY BELCH, SIR ANDREW, and FABIAN] I prithee, gentle friend, Let thy fair wisdom, not thy passion, sway In this uncivil and thou unjust extent Against thy peace. Go with me to my house, And hear thou there how many fruitless pranks This ruffian hath botch'd up, that thou thereby Mayst smile at this: thou shalt not choose but go: Do not deny. Beshrew his soul for me, He started one poor heart of mine in thee.
lines: Will it be ever thus? Ungracious wretch,
Fit for the mountains and the barbarous caves,
Where manners ne'er were preach'd! out of my sight!
Be not offended, dear Cesario.
Rudesby, be gone!
[Exeunt SIR TOBY BELCH, SIR ANDREW, and FABIAN]
I prithee, gentle friend,
Let thy fair wisdom, not thy passion, sway
In this uncivil and thou unjust extent
Against thy peace. Go with me to my house,
And hear thou there how many fruitless pranks
This ruffian hath botch'd up, that thou thereby
Mayst smile at this: thou shalt not choose but go:
Do not deny. Beshrew his soul for me,
He started one poor heart of mine in thee.

speeches.speech: What relish is in this? how runs the stream? Or I am mad, or else this is a dream: Let fancy still my sense in Lethe steep; If it be thus to dream, still let me sleep!
lines: What relish is in this? how runs the stream?
Or I am mad, or else this is a dream:
Let fancy still my sense in Lethe steep;
If it be thus to dream, still let me sleep!

speeches.speech: Nay, come, I prithee; would thou'ldst be ruled by me!
lines: Nay, come, I prithee; would thou'ldst be ruled by me!

speeches.speech: Madam, I will.
lines: Madam, I will.

speeches.speech: O, say so, and so be!
lines: O, say so, and so be!

speeches.speech: Nay, I prithee, put on this gown and this beard; make him believe thou art Sir Topas the curate: do it quickly; I'll call Sir Toby the whilst.
lines: Nay, I prithee, put on this gown and this beard;
make him believe thou art Sir Topas the curate: do
it quickly; I'll call Sir Toby the whilst.

speeches.speech: Well, I'll put it on, and I will dissemble myself in't; and I would I were the first that ever dissembled in such a gown. I am not tall enough to become the function well, nor lean enough to be thought a good student; but to be said an honest man and a good housekeeper goes as fairly as to say a careful man and a great scholar. The competitors enter.
lines: Well, I'll put it on, and I will dissemble myself
in't; and I would I were the first that ever
dissembled in such a gown. I am not tall enough to
become the function well, nor lean enough to be
thought a good student; but to be said an honest man
and a good housekeeper goes as fairly as to say a
careful man and a great scholar. The competitors enter.

speeches.speech: Jove bless thee, master Parson.
lines: Jove bless thee, master Parson.

speeches.speech: Bonos dies, Sir Toby: for, as the old hermit of Prague, that never saw pen and ink, very wittily said to a niece of King Gorboduc, 'That that is is;' so I, being Master Parson, am Master Parson; for, what is 'that' but 'that,' and 'is' but 'is'?
lines: Bonos dies, Sir Toby: for, as the old hermit of
Prague, that never saw pen and ink, very wittily
said to a niece of King Gorboduc, 'That that is is;'
so I, being Master Parson, am Master Parson; for,
what is 'that' but 'that,' and 'is' but 'is'?

speeches.speech: To him, Sir Topas.
lines: To him, Sir Topas.

speeches.speech: What, ho, I say! peace in this prison!
lines: What, ho, I say! peace in this prison!

speeches.speech: The knave counterfeits well; a good knave.
lines: The knave counterfeits well; a good knave.

speeches.speech: [Within] Who calls there?
lines: [Within] Who calls there?

speeches.speech: Sir Topas the curate, who comes to visit Malvolio the lunatic.
lines: Sir Topas the curate, who comes to visit Malvolio
the lunatic.

speeches.speech: Sir Topas, Sir Topas, good Sir Topas, go to my lady.
lines: Sir Topas, Sir Topas, good Sir Topas, go to my lady.

speeches.speech: Out, hyperbolical fiend! how vexest thou this man! talkest thou nothing but of ladies?
lines: Out, hyperbolical fiend! how vexest thou this man!
talkest thou nothing but of ladies?

speeches.speech: Well said, Master Parson.
lines: Well said, Master Parson.

speeches.speech: Sir Topas, never was man thus wronged: good Sir Topas, do not think I am mad: they have laid me here in hideous darkness.
lines: Sir Topas, never was man thus wronged: good Sir
Topas, do not think I am mad: they have laid me
here in hideous darkness.

speeches.speech: Fie, thou dishonest Satan! I call thee by the most modest terms; for I am one of those gentle ones that will use the devil himself with courtesy: sayest thou that house is dark?
lines: Fie, thou dishonest Satan! I call thee by the most
modest terms; for I am one of those gentle ones
that will use the devil himself with courtesy:
sayest thou that house is dark?

speeches.speech: As hell, Sir Topas.
lines: As hell, Sir Topas.

speeches.speech: Why it hath bay windows transparent as barricadoes, and the clearstores toward the south north are as lustrous as ebony; and yet complainest thou of obstruction?
lines: Why it hath bay windows transparent as barricadoes,
and the clearstores toward the south north are as
lustrous as ebony; and yet complainest thou of
obstruction?

speeches.speech: I am not mad, Sir Topas: I say to you, this house is dark.
lines: I am not mad, Sir Topas: I say to you, this house is dark.

speeches.speech: Madman, thou errest: I say, there is no darkness but ignorance; in which thou art more puzzled than the Egyptians in their fog.
lines: Madman, thou errest: I say, there is no darkness
but ignorance; in which thou art more puzzled than
the Egyptians in their fog.

speeches.speech: I say, this house is as dark as ignorance, though ignorance were as dark as hell; and I say, there was never man thus abused. I am no more mad than you are: make the trial of it in any constant question.
lines: I say, this house is as dark as ignorance, though
ignorance were as dark as hell; and I say, there
was never man thus abused. I am no more mad than you
are: make the trial of it in any constant question.

speeches.speech: What is the opinion of Pythagoras concerning wild fowl?
lines: What is the opinion of Pythagoras concerning wild fowl?

speeches.speech: That the soul of our grandam might haply inhabit a bird.
lines: That the soul of our grandam might haply inhabit a bird.

speeches.speech: What thinkest thou of his opinion?
lines: What thinkest thou of his opinion?

speeches.speech: I think nobly of the soul, and no way approve his opinion.
lines: I think nobly of the soul, and no way approve his opinion.

speeches.speech: Fare thee well. Remain thou still in darkness: thou shalt hold the opinion of Pythagoras ere I will allow of thy wits, and fear to kill a woodcock, lest thou dispossess the soul of thy grandam. Fare thee well.
lines: Fare thee well. Remain thou still in darkness:
thou shalt hold the opinion of Pythagoras ere I will
allow of thy wits, and fear to kill a woodcock, lest
thou dispossess the soul of thy grandam. Fare thee well.

speeches.speech: Sir Topas, Sir Topas!
lines: Sir Topas, Sir Topas!

speeches.speech: My most exquisite Sir Topas!
lines: My most exquisite Sir Topas!

speeches.speech: Nay, I am for all waters.
lines: Nay, I am for all waters.

speeches.speech: Thou mightst have done this without thy beard and gown: he sees thee not.
lines: Thou mightst have done this without thy beard and
gown: he sees thee not.

speeches.speech: To him in thine own voice, and bring me word how thou findest him: I would we were well rid of this knavery. If he may be conveniently delivered, I would he were, for I am now so far in offence with my niece that I cannot pursue with any safety this sport to the upshot. Come by and by to my chamber.
lines: To him in thine own voice, and bring me word how
thou findest him: I would we were well rid of this
knavery. If he may be conveniently delivered, I
would he were, for I am now so far in offence with
my niece that I cannot pursue with any safety this
sport to the upshot. Come by and by to my chamber.

speeches.speech: [Singing] 'Hey, Robin, jolly Robin, Tell me how thy lady does.'
lines: [Singing]
'Hey, Robin, jolly Robin,
Tell me how thy lady does.'

speeches.speech: Fool!
lines: Fool!

speeches.speech: 'My lady is unkind, perdy.'
lines: 'My lady is unkind, perdy.'

speeches.speech: Fool!
lines: Fool!

speeches.speech: 'Alas, why is she so?'
lines: 'Alas, why is she so?'

speeches.speech: Fool, I say!
lines: Fool, I say!

speeches.speech: 'She loves another'--Who calls, ha?
lines: 'She loves another'--Who calls, ha?

speeches.speech: Good fool, as ever thou wilt deserve well at my hand, help me to a candle, and pen, ink and paper: as I am a gentleman, I will live to be thankful to thee for't.
lines: Good fool, as ever thou wilt deserve well at my
hand, help me to a candle, and pen, ink and paper:
as I am a gentleman, I will live to be thankful to
thee for't.

speeches.speech: Master Malvolio?
lines: Master Malvolio?

speeches.speech: Ay, good fool.
lines: Ay, good fool.

speeches.speech: Alas, sir, how fell you besides your five wits?
lines: Alas, sir, how fell you besides your five wits?

speeches.speech: Fool, there was never a man so notoriously abused: I am as well in my wits, fool, as thou art.
lines: Fool, there was never a man so notoriously abused: I
am as well in my wits, fool, as thou art.

speeches.speech: But as well? then you are mad indeed, if you be no better in your wits than a fool.
lines: But as well? then you are mad indeed, if you be no
better in your wits than a fool.

speeches.speech: They have here propertied me; keep me in darkness, send ministers to me, asses, and do all they can to face me out of my wits.
lines: They have here propertied me; keep me in darkness,
send ministers to me, asses, and do all they can to
face me out of my wits.

speeches.speech: Advise you what you say; the minister is here. Malvolio, Malvolio, thy wits the heavens restore! endeavour thyself to sleep, and leave thy vain bibble babble.
lines: Advise you what you say; the minister is here.
Malvolio, Malvolio, thy wits the heavens restore!
endeavour thyself to sleep, and leave thy vain
bibble babble.

speeches.speech: Sir Topas!
lines: Sir Topas!

speeches.speech: Maintain no words with him, good fellow. Who, I, sir? not I, sir. God be wi' you, good Sir Topas. Merry, amen. I will, sir, I will.
lines: Maintain no words with him, good fellow. Who, I,
sir? not I, sir. God be wi' you, good Sir Topas.
Merry, amen. I will, sir, I will.

speeches.speech: Fool, fool, fool, I say!
lines: Fool, fool, fool, I say!

speeches.speech: Alas, sir, be patient. What say you sir? I am shent for speaking to you.
lines: Alas, sir, be patient. What say you sir? I am
shent for speaking to you.

speeches.speech: Good fool, help me to some light and some paper: I tell thee, I am as well in my wits as any man in Illyria.
lines: Good fool, help me to some light and some paper: I
tell thee, I am as well in my wits as any man in Illyria.

speeches.speech: Well-a-day that you were, sir
lines: Well-a-day that you were, sir

speeches.speech: By this hand, I am. Good fool, some ink, paper and light; and convey what I will set down to my lady: it shall advantage thee more than ever the bearing of letter did.
lines: By this hand, I am. Good fool, some ink, paper and
light; and convey what I will set down to my lady:
it shall advantage thee more than ever the bearing
of letter did.

speeches.speech: I will help you to't. But tell me true, are you not mad indeed? or do you but counterfeit?
lines: I will help you to't. But tell me true, are you
not mad indeed? or do you but counterfeit?

speeches.speech: Believe me, I am not; I tell thee true.
lines: Believe me, I am not; I tell thee true.

speeches.speech: Nay, I'll ne'er believe a madman till I see his brains. I will fetch you light and paper and ink.
lines: Nay, I'll ne'er believe a madman till I see his
brains. I will fetch you light and paper and ink.

speeches.speech: Fool, I'll requite it in the highest degree: I prithee, be gone.
lines: Fool, I'll requite it in the highest degree: I
prithee, be gone.

speeches.speech: [Singing] I am gone, sir, And anon, sir, I'll be with you again, In a trice, Like to the old Vice, Your need to sustain; Who, with dagger of lath, In his rage and his wrath, Cries, ah, ha! to the devil: Like a mad lad, Pare thy nails, dad; Adieu, good man devil.
lines: [Singing]
I am gone, sir,
And anon, sir,
I'll be with you again,
In a trice,
Like to the old Vice,
Your need to sustain;
Who, with dagger of lath,
In his rage and his wrath,
Cries, ah, ha! to the devil:
Like a mad lad,
Pare thy nails, dad;
Adieu, good man devil.

speeches.speech: This is the air; that is the glorious sun; This pearl she gave me, I do feel't and see't; And though 'tis wonder that enwraps me thus, Yet 'tis not madness. Where's Antonio, then? I could not find him at the Elephant: Yet there he was; and there I found this credit, That he did range the town to seek me out. His counsel now might do me golden service; For though my soul disputes well with my sense, That this may be some error, but no madness, Yet doth this accident and flood of fortune So far exceed all instance, all discourse, That I am ready to distrust mine eyes And wrangle with my reason that persuades me To any other trust but that I am mad Or else the lady's mad; yet, if 'twere so, She could not sway her house, command her followers, Take and give back affairs and their dispatch With such a smooth, discreet and stable bearing As I perceive she does: there's something in't That is deceiveable. But here the lady comes.
lines: This is the air; that is the glorious sun;
This pearl she gave me, I do feel't and see't;
And though 'tis wonder that enwraps me thus,
Yet 'tis not madness. Where's Antonio, then?
I could not find him at the Elephant:
Yet there he was; and there I found this credit,
That he did range the town to seek me out.
His counsel now might do me golden service;
For though my soul disputes well with my sense,
That this may be some error, but no madness,
Yet doth this accident and flood of fortune
So far exceed all instance, all discourse,
That I am ready to distrust mine eyes
And wrangle with my reason that persuades me
To any other trust but that I am mad
Or else the lady's mad; yet, if 'twere so,
She could not sway her house, command her followers,
Take and give back affairs and their dispatch
With such a smooth, discreet and stable bearing
As I perceive she does: there's something in't
That is deceiveable. But here the lady comes.

speeches.speech: Blame not this haste of mine. If you mean well, Now go with me and with this holy man Into the chantry by: there, before him, And underneath that consecrated roof, Plight me the full assurance of your faith; That my most jealous and too doubtful soul May live at peace. He shall conceal it Whiles you are willing it shall come to note, What time we will our celebration keep According to my birth. What do you say?
lines: Blame not this haste of mine. If you mean well,
Now go with me and with this holy man
Into the chantry by: there, before him,
And underneath that consecrated roof,
Plight me the full assurance of your faith;
That my most jealous and too doubtful soul
May live at peace. He shall conceal it
Whiles you are willing it shall come to note,
What time we will our celebration keep
According to my birth. What do you say?

speeches.speech: I'll follow this good man, and go with you; And, having sworn truth, ever will be true.
lines: I'll follow this good man, and go with you;
And, having sworn truth, ever will be true.

speeches.speech: Then lead the way, good father; and heavens so shine, That they may fairly note this act of mine!
lines: Then lead the way, good father; and heavens so shine,
That they may fairly note this act of mine!

speeches.speech: Now, as thou lovest me, let me see his letter.
lines: Now, as thou lovest me, let me see his letter.

speeches.speech: Good Master Fabian, grant me another request.
lines: Good Master Fabian, grant me another request.

speeches.speech: Any thing.
lines: Any thing.

speeches.speech: Do not desire to see this letter.
lines: Do not desire to see this letter.

speeches.speech: This is, to give a dog, and in recompense desire my dog again.
lines: This is, to give a dog, and in recompense desire my
dog again.

speeches.speech: Belong you to the Lady Olivia, friends?
lines: Belong you to the Lady Olivia, friends?

speeches.speech: Ay, sir; we are some of her trappings.
lines: Ay, sir; we are some of her trappings.

speeches.speech: I know thee well; how dost thou, my good fellow?
lines: I know thee well; how dost thou, my good fellow?

speeches.speech: Truly, sir, the better for my foes and the worse for my friends.
lines: Truly, sir, the better for my foes and the worse
for my friends.

speeches.speech: Just the contrary; the better for thy friends.
lines: Just the contrary; the better for thy friends.

speeches.speech: No, sir, the worse.
lines: No, sir, the worse.

speeches.speech: How can that be?
lines: How can that be?

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, they praise me and make an ass of me; now my foes tell me plainly I am an ass: so that by my foes, sir I profit in the knowledge of myself, and by my friends, I am abused: so that, conclusions to be as kisses, if your four negatives make your two affirmatives why then, the worse for my friends and the better for my foes.
lines: Marry, sir, they praise me and make an ass of me;
now my foes tell me plainly I am an ass: so that by
my foes, sir I profit in the knowledge of myself,
and by my friends, I am abused: so that,
conclusions to be as kisses, if your four negatives
make your two affirmatives why then, the worse for
my friends and the better for my foes.

speeches.speech: Why, this is excellent.
lines: Why, this is excellent.

speeches.speech: By my troth, sir, no; though it please you to be one of my friends.
lines: By my troth, sir, no; though it please you to be
one of my friends.

speeches.speech: Thou shalt not be the worse for me: there's gold.
lines: Thou shalt not be the worse for me: there's gold.

speeches.speech: But that it would be double-dealing, sir, I would you could make it another.
lines: But that it would be double-dealing, sir, I would
you could make it another.

speeches.speech: O, you give me ill counsel.
lines: O, you give me ill counsel.

speeches.speech: Put your grace in your pocket, sir, for this once, and let your flesh and blood obey it.
lines: Put your grace in your pocket, sir, for this once,
and let your flesh and blood obey it.

speeches.speech: Well, I will be so much a sinner, to be a double-dealer: there's another.
lines: Well, I will be so much a sinner, to be a
double-dealer: there's another.

speeches.speech: Primo, secundo, tertio, is a good play; and the old saying is, the third pays for all: the triplex, sir, is a good tripping measure; or the bells of Saint Bennet, sir, may put you in mind; one, two, three.
lines: Primo, secundo, tertio, is a good play; and the old
saying is, the third pays for all: the triplex,
sir, is a good tripping measure; or the bells of
Saint Bennet, sir, may put you in mind; one, two, three.

speeches.speech: You can fool no more money out of me at this throw: if you will let your lady know I am here to speak with her, and bring her along with you, it may awake my bounty further.
lines: You can fool no more money out of me at this throw:
if you will let your lady know I am here to speak
with her, and bring her along with you, it may awake
my bounty further.

speeches.speech: Marry, sir, lullaby to your bounty till I come again. I go, sir; but I would not have you to think that my desire of having is the sin of covetousness: but, as you say, sir, let your bounty take a nap, I will awake it anon.
lines: Marry, sir, lullaby to your bounty till I come
again. I go, sir; but I would not have you to think
that my desire of having is the sin of covetousness:
but, as you say, sir, let your bounty take a nap, I
will awake it anon.

speeches.speech: Here comes the man, sir, that did rescue me.
lines: Here comes the man, sir, that did rescue me.

speeches.speech: That face of his I do remember well; Yet, when I saw it last, it was besmear'd As black as Vulcan in the smoke of war: A bawbling vessel was he captain of, For shallow draught and bulk unprizable; With which such scathful grapple did he make With the most noble bottom of our fleet, That very envy and the tongue of loss Cried fame and honour on him. What's the matter?
lines: That face of his I do remember well;
Yet, when I saw it last, it was besmear'd
As black as Vulcan in the smoke of war:
A bawbling vessel was he captain of,
For shallow draught and bulk unprizable;
With which such scathful grapple did he make
With the most noble bottom of our fleet,
That very envy and the tongue of loss
Cried fame and honour on him. What's the matter?

speeches.speech: Orsino, this is that Antonio That took the Phoenix and her fraught from Candy; And this is he that did the Tiger board, When your young nephew Titus lost his leg: Here in the streets, desperate of shame and state, In private brabble did we apprehend him.
lines: Orsino, this is that Antonio
That took the Phoenix and her fraught from Candy;
And this is he that did the Tiger board,
When your young nephew Titus lost his leg:
Here in the streets, desperate of shame and state,
In private brabble did we apprehend him.

speeches.speech: He did me kindness, sir, drew on my side; But in conclusion put strange speech upon me: I know not what 'twas but distraction.
lines: He did me kindness, sir, drew on my side;
But in conclusion put strange speech upon me:
I know not what 'twas but distraction.

speeches.speech: Notable pirate! thou salt-water thief! What foolish boldness brought thee to their mercies, Whom thou, in terms so bloody and so dear, Hast made thine enemies?
lines: Notable pirate! thou salt-water thief!
What foolish boldness brought thee to their mercies,
Whom thou, in terms so bloody and so dear,
Hast made thine enemies?

speeches.speech: Orsino, noble sir, Be pleased that I shake off these names you give me: Antonio never yet was thief or pirate, Though I confess, on base and ground enough, Orsino's enemy. A witchcraft drew me hither: That most ingrateful boy there by your side, From the rude sea's enraged and foamy mouth Did I redeem; a wreck past hope he was: His life I gave him and did thereto add My love, without retention or restraint, All his in dedication; for his sake Did I expose myself, pure for his love, Into the danger of this adverse town; Drew to defend him when he was beset: Where being apprehended, his false cunning, Not meaning to partake with me in danger, Taught him to face me out of his acquaintance, And grew a twenty years removed thing While one would wink; denied me mine own purse, Which I had recommended to his use Not half an hour before.
lines: Orsino, noble sir,
Be pleased that I shake off these names you give me:
Antonio never yet was thief or pirate,
Though I confess, on base and ground enough,
Orsino's enemy. A witchcraft drew me hither:
That most ingrateful boy there by your side,
From the rude sea's enraged and foamy mouth
Did I redeem; a wreck past hope he was:
His life I gave him and did thereto add
My love, without retention or restraint,
All his in dedication; for his sake
Did I expose myself, pure for his love,
Into the danger of this adverse town;
Drew to defend him when he was beset:
Where being apprehended, his false cunning,
Not meaning to partake with me in danger,
Taught him to face me out of his acquaintance,
And grew a twenty years removed thing
While one would wink; denied me mine own purse,
Which I had recommended to his use
Not half an hour before.

speeches.speech: How can this be?
lines: How can this be?

speeches.speech: When came he to this town?
lines: When came he to this town?

speeches.speech: To-day, my lord; and for three months before, No interim, not a minute's vacancy, Both day and night did we keep company.
lines: To-day, my lord; and for three months before,
No interim, not a minute's vacancy,
Both day and night did we keep company.

speeches.speech: Here comes the countess: now heaven walks on earth. But for thee, fellow; fellow, thy words are madness: Three months this youth hath tended upon me; But more of that anon. Take him aside.
lines: Here comes the countess: now heaven walks on earth.
But for thee, fellow; fellow, thy words are madness:
Three months this youth hath tended upon me;
But more of that anon. Take him aside.

speeches.speech: What would my lord, but that he may not have, Wherein Olivia may seem serviceable? Cesario, you do not keep promise with me.
lines: What would my lord, but that he may not have,
Wherein Olivia may seem serviceable?
Cesario, you do not keep promise with me.

speeches.speech: Madam!
lines: Madam!

speeches.speech: Gracious Olivia,--
lines: Gracious Olivia,--

speeches.speech: What do you say, Cesario? Good my lord,--
lines: What do you say, Cesario? Good my lord,--

speeches.speech: My lord would speak; my duty hushes me.
lines: My lord would speak; my duty hushes me.

speeches.speech: If it be aught to the old tune, my lord, It is as fat and fulsome to mine ear As howling after music.
lines: If it be aught to the old tune, my lord,
It is as fat and fulsome to mine ear
As howling after music.

speeches.speech: Still so cruel?
lines: Still so cruel?

speeches.speech: Still so constant, lord.
lines: Still so constant, lord.

speeches.speech: What, to perverseness? you uncivil lady, To whose ingrate and unauspicious altars My soul the faithfull'st offerings hath breathed out That e'er devotion tender'd! What shall I do?
lines: What, to perverseness? you uncivil lady,
To whose ingrate and unauspicious altars
My soul the faithfull'st offerings hath breathed out
That e'er devotion tender'd! What shall I do?

speeches.speech: Even what it please my lord, that shall become him.
lines: Even what it please my lord, that shall become him.

speeches.speech: Why should I not, had I the heart to do it, Like to the Egyptian thief at point of death, Kill what I love?--a savage jealousy That sometimes savours nobly. But hear me this: Since you to non-regardance cast my faith, And that I partly know the instrument That screws me from my true place in your favour, Live you the marble-breasted tyrant still; But this your minion, whom I know you love, And whom, by heaven I swear, I tender dearly, Him will I tear out of that cruel eye, Where he sits crowned in his master's spite. Come, boy, with me; my thoughts are ripe in mischief: I'll sacrifice the lamb that I do love, To spite a raven's heart within a dove.
lines: Why should I not, had I the heart to do it,
Like to the Egyptian thief at point of death,
Kill what I love?--a savage jealousy
That sometimes savours nobly. But hear me this:
Since you to non-regardance cast my faith,
And that I partly know the instrument
That screws me from my true place in your favour,
Live you the marble-breasted tyrant still;
But this your minion, whom I know you love,
And whom, by heaven I swear, I tender dearly,
Him will I tear out of that cruel eye,
Where he sits crowned in his master's spite.
Come, boy, with me; my thoughts are ripe in mischief:
I'll sacrifice the lamb that I do love,
To spite a raven's heart within a dove.

speeches.speech: And I, most jocund, apt and willingly, To do you rest, a thousand deaths would die.
lines: And I, most jocund, apt and willingly,
To do you rest, a thousand deaths would die.

speeches.speech: Where goes Cesario?
lines: Where goes Cesario?

speeches.speech: After him I love More than I love these eyes, more than my life, More, by all mores, than e'er I shall love wife. If I do feign, you witnesses above Punish my life for tainting of my love!
lines: After him I love
More than I love these eyes, more than my life,
More, by all mores, than e'er I shall love wife.
If I do feign, you witnesses above
Punish my life for tainting of my love!

speeches.speech: Ay me, detested! how am I beguiled!
lines: Ay me, detested! how am I beguiled!

speeches.speech: Who does beguile you? who does do you wrong?
lines: Who does beguile you? who does do you wrong?

speeches.speech: Hast thou forgot thyself? is it so long? Call forth the holy father.
lines: Hast thou forgot thyself? is it so long?
Call forth the holy father.

speeches.speech: Come, away!
lines: Come, away!

speeches.speech: Whither, my lord? Cesario, husband, stay.
lines: Whither, my lord? Cesario, husband, stay.

speeches.speech: Husband!
lines: Husband!

speeches.speech: Ay, husband: can he that deny?
lines: Ay, husband: can he that deny?

speeches.speech: Her husband, sirrah!
lines: Her husband, sirrah!

speeches.speech: No, my lord, not I.
lines: No, my lord, not I.

speeches.speech: Alas, it is the baseness of thy fear That makes thee strangle thy propriety: Fear not, Cesario; take thy fortunes up; Be that thou know'st thou art, and then thou art As great as that thou fear'st. [Enter Priest] O, welcome, father! Father, I charge thee, by thy reverence, Here to unfold, though lately we intended To keep in darkness what occasion now Reveals before 'tis ripe, what thou dost know Hath newly pass'd between this youth and me.
lines: Alas, it is the baseness of thy fear
That makes thee strangle thy propriety:
Fear not, Cesario; take thy fortunes up;
Be that thou know'st thou art, and then thou art
As great as that thou fear'st.
[Enter Priest]
O, welcome, father!
Father, I charge thee, by thy reverence,
Here to unfold, though lately we intended
To keep in darkness what occasion now
Reveals before 'tis ripe, what thou dost know
Hath newly pass'd between this youth and me.

speeches.speech: A contract of eternal bond of love, Confirm'd by mutual joinder of your hands, Attested by the holy close of lips, Strengthen'd by interchangement of your rings; And all the ceremony of this compact Seal'd in my function, by my testimony: Since when, my watch hath told me, toward my grave I have travell'd but two hours.
lines: A contract of eternal bond of love,
Confirm'd by mutual joinder of your hands,
Attested by the holy close of lips,
Strengthen'd by interchangement of your rings;
And all the ceremony of this compact
Seal'd in my function, by my testimony:
Since when, my watch hath told me, toward my grave
I have travell'd but two hours.

speeches.speech: O thou dissembling cub! what wilt thou be When time hath sow'd a grizzle on thy case? Or will not else thy craft so quickly grow, That thine own trip shall be thine overthrow? Farewell, and take her; but direct thy feet Where thou and I henceforth may never meet.
lines: O thou dissembling cub! what wilt thou be
When time hath sow'd a grizzle on thy case?
Or will not else thy craft so quickly grow,
That thine own trip shall be thine overthrow?
Farewell, and take her; but direct thy feet
Where thou and I henceforth may never meet.

speeches.speech: My lord, I do protest--
lines: My lord, I do protest--

speeches.speech: O, do not swear! Hold little faith, though thou hast too much fear.
lines: O, do not swear!
Hold little faith, though thou hast too much fear.

speeches.speech: For the love of God, a surgeon! Send one presently to Sir Toby.
lines: For the love of God, a surgeon! Send one presently
to Sir Toby.

speeches.speech: What's the matter?
lines: What's the matter?

speeches.speech: He has broke my head across and has given Sir Toby a bloody coxcomb too: for the love of God, your help! I had rather than forty pound I were at home.
lines: He has broke my head across and has given Sir Toby
a bloody coxcomb too: for the love of God, your
help! I had rather than forty pound I were at home.

speeches.speech: Who has done this, Sir Andrew?
lines: Who has done this, Sir Andrew?

speeches.speech: The count's gentleman, one Cesario: we took him for a coward, but he's the very devil incardinate.
lines: The count's gentleman, one Cesario: we took him for
a coward, but he's the very devil incardinate.

speeches.speech: My gentleman, Cesario?
lines: My gentleman, Cesario?

speeches.speech: 'Od's lifelings, here he is! You broke my head for nothing; and that that I did, I was set on to do't by Sir Toby.
lines: 'Od's lifelings, here he is! You broke my head for
nothing; and that that I did, I was set on to do't
by Sir Toby.

speeches.speech: Why do you speak to me? I never hurt you: You drew your sword upon me without cause; But I bespoke you fair, and hurt you not.
lines: Why do you speak to me? I never hurt you:
You drew your sword upon me without cause;
But I bespoke you fair, and hurt you not.

speeches.speech: If a bloody coxcomb be a hurt, you have hurt me: I think you set nothing by a bloody coxcomb. [Enter SIR TOBY BELCH and Clown] Here comes Sir Toby halting; you shall hear more: but if he had not been in drink, he would have tickled you othergates than he did.
lines: If a bloody coxcomb be a hurt, you have hurt me: I
think you set nothing by a bloody coxcomb.
[Enter SIR TOBY BELCH and Clown]
Here comes Sir Toby halting; you shall hear more:
but if he had not been in drink, he would have
tickled you othergates than he did.

speeches.speech: How now, gentleman! how is't with you?
lines: How now, gentleman! how is't with you?

speeches.speech: That's all one: has hurt me, and there's the end on't. Sot, didst see Dick surgeon, sot?
lines: That's all one: has hurt me, and there's the end
on't. Sot, didst see Dick surgeon, sot?

speeches.speech: O, he's drunk, Sir Toby, an hour agone; his eyes were set at eight i' the morning.
lines: O, he's drunk, Sir Toby, an hour agone; his eyes
were set at eight i' the morning.

speeches.speech: Then he's a rogue, and a passy measures panyn: I hate a drunken rogue.
lines: Then he's a rogue, and a passy measures panyn: I
hate a drunken rogue.

speeches.speech: Away with him! Who hath made this havoc with them?
lines: Away with him! Who hath made this havoc with them?

speeches.speech: I'll help you, Sir Toby, because well be dressed together.
lines: I'll help you, Sir Toby, because well be dressed together.

speeches.speech: Will you help? an ass-head and a coxcomb and a knave, a thin-faced knave, a gull!
lines: Will you help? an ass-head and a coxcomb and a
knave, a thin-faced knave, a gull!

speeches.speech: Get him to bed, and let his hurt be look'd to.
lines: Get him to bed, and let his hurt be look'd to.

speeches.speech: I am sorry, madam, I have hurt your kinsman: But, had it been the brother of my blood, I must have done no less with wit and safety. You throw a strange regard upon me, and by that I do perceive it hath offended you: Pardon me, sweet one, even for the vows We made each other but so late ago.
lines: I am sorry, madam, I have hurt your kinsman:
But, had it been the brother of my blood,
I must have done no less with wit and safety.
You throw a strange regard upon me, and by that
I do perceive it hath offended you:
Pardon me, sweet one, even for the vows
We made each other but so late ago.

speeches.speech: One face, one voice, one habit, and two persons, A natural perspective, that is and is not!
lines: One face, one voice, one habit, and two persons,
A natural perspective, that is and is not!

speeches.speech: Antonio, O my dear Antonio! How have the hours rack'd and tortured me, Since I have lost thee!
lines: Antonio, O my dear Antonio!
How have the hours rack'd and tortured me,
Since I have lost thee!

speeches.speech: Sebastian are you?
lines: Sebastian are you?

speeches.speech: Fear'st thou that, Antonio?
lines: Fear'st thou that, Antonio?

speeches.speech: How have you made division of yourself? An apple, cleft in two, is not more twin Than these two creatures. Which is Sebastian?
lines: How have you made division of yourself?
An apple, cleft in two, is not more twin
Than these two creatures. Which is Sebastian?

speeches.speech: Most wonderful!
lines: Most wonderful!

speeches.speech: Do I stand there? I never had a brother; Nor can there be that deity in my nature, Of here and every where. I had a sister, Whom the blind waves and surges have devour'd. Of charity, what kin are you to me? What countryman? what name? what parentage?
lines: Do I stand there? I never had a brother;
Nor can there be that deity in my nature,
Of here and every where. I had a sister,
Whom the blind waves and surges have devour'd.
Of charity, what kin are you to me?
What countryman? what name? what parentage?

speeches.speech: Of Messaline: Sebastian was my father; Such a Sebastian was my brother too, So went he suited to his watery tomb: If spirits can assume both form and suit You come to fright us.
lines: Of Messaline: Sebastian was my father;
Such a Sebastian was my brother too,
So went he suited to his watery tomb:
If spirits can assume both form and suit
You come to fright us.

speeches.speech: A spirit I am indeed; But am in that dimension grossly clad Which from the womb I did participate. Were you a woman, as the rest goes even, I should my tears let fall upon your cheek, And say 'Thrice-welcome, drowned Viola!'
lines: A spirit I am indeed;
But am in that dimension grossly clad
Which from the womb I did participate.
Were you a woman, as the rest goes even,
I should my tears let fall upon your cheek,
And say 'Thrice-welcome, drowned Viola!'

speeches.speech: My father had a mole upon his brow.
lines: My father had a mole upon his brow.

speeches.speech: And so had mine.
lines: And so had mine.

speeches.speech: And died that day when Viola from her birth Had number'd thirteen years.
lines: And died that day when Viola from her birth
Had number'd thirteen years.

speeches.speech: O, that record is lively in my soul! He finished indeed his mortal act That day that made my sister thirteen years.
lines: O, that record is lively in my soul!
He finished indeed his mortal act
That day that made my sister thirteen years.

speeches.speech: If nothing lets to make us happy both But this my masculine usurp'd attire, Do not embrace me till each circumstance Of place, time, fortune, do cohere and jump That I am Viola: which to confirm, I'll bring you to a captain in this town, Where lie my maiden weeds; by whose gentle help I was preserved to serve this noble count. All the occurrence of my fortune since Hath been between this lady and this lord.
lines: If nothing lets to make us happy both
But this my masculine usurp'd attire,
Do not embrace me till each circumstance
Of place, time, fortune, do cohere and jump
That I am Viola: which to confirm,
I'll bring you to a captain in this town,
Where lie my maiden weeds; by whose gentle help
I was preserved to serve this noble count.
All the occurrence of my fortune since
Hath been between this lady and this lord.

speeches.speech: [To OLIVIA] So comes it, lady, you have been mistook: But nature to her bias drew in that. You would have been contracted to a maid; Nor are you therein, by my life, deceived, You are betroth'd both to a maid and man.
lines: [To OLIVIA] So comes it, lady, you have been mistook:
But nature to her bias drew in that.
You would have been contracted to a maid;
Nor are you therein, by my life, deceived,
You are betroth'd both to a maid and man.

speeches.speech: Be not amazed; right noble is his blood. If this be so, as yet the glass seems true, I shall have share in this most happy wreck. [To VIOLA] Boy, thou hast said to me a thousand times Thou never shouldst love woman like to me.
lines: Be not amazed; right noble is his blood.
If this be so, as yet the glass seems true,
I shall have share in this most happy wreck.
[To VIOLA]
Boy, thou hast said to me a thousand times
Thou never shouldst love woman like to me.

speeches.speech: And all those sayings will I overswear; And those swearings keep as true in soul As doth that orbed continent the fire That severs day from night.
lines: And all those sayings will I overswear;
And those swearings keep as true in soul
As doth that orbed continent the fire
That severs day from night.

speeches.speech: Give me thy hand; And let me see thee in thy woman's weeds.
lines: Give me thy hand;
And let me see thee in thy woman's weeds.

speeches.speech: The captain that did bring me first on shore Hath my maid's garments: he upon some action Is now in durance, at Malvolio's suit, A gentleman, and follower of my lady's.
lines: The captain that did bring me first on shore
Hath my maid's garments: he upon some action
Is now in durance, at Malvolio's suit,
A gentleman, and follower of my lady's.

speeches.speech: He shall enlarge him: fetch Malvolio hither: And yet, alas, now I remember me, They say, poor gentleman, he's much distract. [Re-enter Clown with a letter, and FABIAN] A most extracting frenzy of mine own From my remembrance clearly banish'd his. How does he, sirrah?
lines: He shall enlarge him: fetch Malvolio hither:
And yet, alas, now I remember me,
They say, poor gentleman, he's much distract.
[Re-enter Clown with a letter, and FABIAN]
A most extracting frenzy of mine own
From my remembrance clearly banish'd his.
How does he, sirrah?

speeches.speech: Truly, madam, he holds Belzebub at the staves's end as well as a man in his case may do: has here writ a letter to you; I should have given't you to-day morning, but as a madman's epistles are no gospels, so it skills not much when they are delivered.
lines: Truly, madam, he holds Belzebub at the staves's end as
well as a man in his case may do: has here writ a
letter to you; I should have given't you to-day
morning, but as a madman's epistles are no gospels,
so it skills not much when they are delivered.

speeches.speech: Open't, and read it.
lines: Open't, and read it.

speeches.speech: Look then to be well edified when the fool delivers the madman. [Reads] 'By the Lord, madam,'--
lines: Look then to be well edified when the fool delivers
the madman.
[Reads]
'By the Lord, madam,'--

speeches.speech: How now! art thou mad?
lines: How now! art thou mad?

speeches.speech: No, madam, I do but read madness: an your ladyship will have it as it ought to be, you must allow Vox.
lines: No, madam, I do but read madness: an your ladyship
will have it as it ought to be, you must allow Vox.

speeches.speech: Prithee, read i' thy right wits.
lines: Prithee, read i' thy right wits.

speeches.speech: So I do, madonna; but to read his right wits is to read thus: therefore perpend, my princess, and give ear.
lines: So I do, madonna; but to read his right wits is to
read thus: therefore perpend, my princess, and give ear.

speeches.speech: Read it you, sirrah.
lines: Read it you, sirrah.

speeches.speech: [Reads] 'By the Lord, madam, you wrong me, and the world shall know it: though you have put me into darkness and given your drunken cousin rule over me, yet have I the benefit of my senses as well as your ladyship. I have your own letter that induced me to the semblance I put on; with the which I doubt not but to do myself much right, or you much shame. Think of me as you please. I leave my duty a little unthought of and speak out of my injury. THE MADLY-USED MALVOLIO.'
lines: [Reads] 'By the Lord, madam, you wrong me, and the
world shall know it: though you have put me into
darkness and given your drunken cousin rule over
me, yet have I the benefit of my senses as well as
your ladyship. I have your own letter that induced
me to the semblance I put on; with the which I doubt
not but to do myself much right, or you much shame.
Think of me as you please. I leave my duty a little
unthought of and speak out of my injury.
THE MADLY-USED MALVOLIO.'

speeches.speech: Did he write this?
lines: Did he write this?

speeches.speech: Ay, madam.
lines: Ay, madam.

speeches.speech: This savours not much of distraction.
lines: This savours not much of distraction.

speeches.speech: See him deliver'd, Fabian; bring him hither. [Exit FABIAN] My lord so please you, these things further thought on, To think me as well a sister as a wife, One day shall crown the alliance on't, so please you, Here at my house and at my proper cost.
lines: See him deliver'd, Fabian; bring him hither.
[Exit FABIAN]
My lord so please you, these things further
thought on,
To think me as well a sister as a wife,
One day shall crown the alliance on't, so please you,
Here at my house and at my proper cost.

speeches.speech: Madam, I am most apt to embrace your offer. [To VIOLA] Your master quits you; and for your service done him, So much against the mettle of your sex, So far beneath your soft and tender breeding, And since you call'd me master for so long, Here is my hand: you shall from this time be Your master's mistress.
lines: Madam, I am most apt to embrace your offer.
[To VIOLA]
Your master quits you; and for your service done him,
So much against the mettle of your sex,
So far beneath your soft and tender breeding,
And since you call'd me master for so long,
Here is my hand: you shall from this time be
Your master's mistress.

speeches.speech: A sister! you are she.
lines: A sister! you are she.

speeches.speech: Is this the madman?
lines: Is this the madman?

speeches.speech: Ay, my lord, this same. How now, Malvolio!
lines: Ay, my lord, this same.
How now, Malvolio!

speeches.speech: Madam, you have done me wrong, Notorious wrong.
lines: Madam, you have done me wrong,
Notorious wrong.

speeches.speech: Have I, Malvolio? no.
lines: Have I, Malvolio? no.

speeches.speech: Lady, you have. Pray you, peruse that letter. You must not now deny it is your hand: Write from it, if you can, in hand or phrase; Or say 'tis not your seal, nor your invention: You can say none of this: well, grant it then And tell me, in the modesty of honour, Why you have given me such clear lights of favour, Bade me come smiling and cross-garter'd to you, To put on yellow stockings and to frown Upon Sir Toby and the lighter people; And, acting this in an obedient hope, Why have you suffer'd me to be imprison'd, Kept in a dark house, visited by the priest, And made the most notorious geck and gull That e'er invention play'd on? tell me why.
lines: Lady, you have. Pray you, peruse that letter.
You must not now deny it is your hand:
Write from it, if you can, in hand or phrase;
Or say 'tis not your seal, nor your invention:
You can say none of this: well, grant it then
And tell me, in the modesty of honour,
Why you have given me such clear lights of favour,
Bade me come smiling and cross-garter'd to you,
To put on yellow stockings and to frown
Upon Sir Toby and the lighter people;
And, acting this in an obedient hope,
Why have you suffer'd me to be imprison'd,
Kept in a dark house, visited by the priest,
And made the most notorious geck and gull
That e'er invention play'd on? tell me why.

speeches.speech: Alas, Malvolio, this is not my writing, Though, I confess, much like the character But out of question 'tis Maria's hand. And now I do bethink me, it was she First told me thou wast mad; then camest in smiling, And in such forms which here were presupposed Upon thee in the letter. Prithee, be content: This practise hath most shrewdly pass'd upon thee; But when we know the grounds and authors of it, Thou shalt be both the plaintiff and the judge Of thine own cause.
lines: Alas, Malvolio, this is not my writing,
Though, I confess, much like the character
But out of question 'tis Maria's hand.
And now I do bethink me, it was she
First told me thou wast mad; then camest in smiling,
And in such forms which here were presupposed
Upon thee in the letter. Prithee, be content:
This practise hath most shrewdly pass'd upon thee;
But when we know the grounds and authors of it,
Thou shalt be both the plaintiff and the judge
Of thine own cause.

speeches.speech: Good madam, hear me speak, And let no quarrel nor no brawl to come Taint the condition of this present hour, Which I have wonder'd at. In hope it shall not, Most freely I confess, myself and Toby Set this device against Malvolio here, Upon some stubborn and uncourteous parts We had conceived against him: Maria writ The letter at Sir Toby's great importance; In recompense whereof he hath married her. How with a sportful malice it was follow'd, May rather pluck on laughter than revenge; If that the injuries be justly weigh'd That have on both sides pass'd.
lines: Good madam, hear me speak,
And let no quarrel nor no brawl to come
Taint the condition of this present hour,
Which I have wonder'd at. In hope it shall not,
Most freely I confess, myself and Toby
Set this device against Malvolio here,
Upon some stubborn and uncourteous parts
We had conceived against him: Maria writ
The letter at Sir Toby's great importance;
In recompense whereof he hath married her.
How with a sportful malice it was follow'd,
May rather pluck on laughter than revenge;
If that the injuries be justly weigh'd
That have on both sides pass'd.

speeches.speech: Alas, poor fool, how have they baffled thee!
lines: Alas, poor fool, how have they baffled thee!

speeches.speech: Why, 'some are born great, some achieve greatness, and some have greatness thrown upon them.' I was one, sir, in this interlude; one Sir Topas, sir; but that's all one. 'By the Lord, fool, I am not mad.' But do you remember? 'Madam, why laugh you at such a barren rascal? an you smile not, he's gagged:' and thus the whirligig of time brings in his revenges.
lines: Why, 'some are born great, some achieve greatness,
and some have greatness thrown upon them.' I was
one, sir, in this interlude; one Sir Topas, sir; but
that's all one. 'By the Lord, fool, I am not mad.'
But do you remember? 'Madam, why laugh you at such
a barren rascal? an you smile not, he's gagged:'
and thus the whirligig of time brings in his revenges.

speeches.speech: I'll be revenged on the whole pack of you.
lines: I'll be revenged on the whole pack of you.

speeches.speech: He hath been most notoriously abused.
lines: He hath been most notoriously abused.

speeches.speech: Pursue him and entreat him to a peace: He hath not told us of the captain yet: When that is known and golden time convents, A solemn combination shall be made Of our dear souls. Meantime, sweet sister, We will not part from hence. Cesario, come; For so you shall be, while you are a man; But when in other habits you are seen, Orsino's mistress and his fancy's queen.
lines: Pursue him and entreat him to a peace:
He hath not told us of the captain yet:
When that is known and golden time convents,
A solemn combination shall be made
Of our dear souls. Meantime, sweet sister,
We will not part from hence. Cesario, come;
For so you shall be, while you are a man;
But when in other habits you are seen,
Orsino's mistress and his fancy's queen.

speeches.speech: [Sings] When that I was and a little tiny boy, With hey, ho, the wind and the rain, A foolish thing was but a toy, For the rain it raineth every day. But when I came to man's estate, With hey, ho, &c. 'Gainst knaves and thieves men shut their gate, For the rain, &c. But when I came, alas! to wive, With hey, ho, &c. By swaggering could I never thrive, For the rain, &c. But when I came unto my beds, With hey, ho, &c. With toss-pots still had drunken heads, For the rain, &c. A great while ago the world begun, With hey, ho, &c. But that's all one, our play is done, And we'll strive to please you every day.
lines: [Sings]
When that I was and a little tiny boy,
With hey, ho, the wind and the rain,
A foolish thing was but a toy,
For the rain it raineth every day.
But when I came to man's estate,
With hey, ho, &c.
'Gainst knaves and thieves men shut their gate,
For the rain, &c.
But when I came, alas! to wive,
With hey, ho, &c.
By swaggering could I never thrive,
For the rain, &c.
But when I came unto my beds,
With hey, ho, &c.
With toss-pots still had drunken heads,
For the rain, &c.
A great while ago the world begun,
With hey, ho, &c.
But that's all one, our play is done,
And we'll strive to please you every day.
